results.question: A 48-year-ol d man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 45 minutes after he collapsed at home. He did not have loss of consciousness. He has a 6-week history of shortness of breath and severe fatigue when he walks 20 to 30 feet. During the past 6 weeks, he also has had a 5-kg (11-lb) weight gain despite a decreased appetite. He has no history of serious illness. He cannot remember his last visit to a physician. His only medication is ibuprofen for intermittent mild headaches. His father died of “heart problems” at the age of 78 years. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 22 years. He has consumed 24 cans of beer weekly for 20 years. He works as a painting and drywall contractor but was unable to work last week because of fatigue. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 100/min and regular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 90/55 mm Hg while supine. Pulse oximetry on 4 L/min of oxygen by nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 91%. On examination, crackles are heard halfway up the lung fields bilaterally. An S 3 is heard; no murmurs are heard. The liver span is 5 cm. There is moderate pitting edema of the calves and ankle swelling bilaterally. Echocardiography is most likely to show which of the following findings?
results.response:
Based on the patient's presentation, there is a 6-week history of progressive shortness of breath, severe fatigue on minimal exertion, weight gain (likely due to fluid retention), decreased appetite, and an inability to work. Physical exam reveals hypotension, hypoxia, crackles bilaterally (suggesting pulmonary edema), an S3 gallop (indicative of heart failure), hepatomegaly (liver span 5 cm), and bilateral pitting edema. These findings are consistent with acute decompensated heart failure. His history includes significant alcohol use (24 cans of beer weekly for 20 years), which is a known risk factor for dilated cardiomyopathy. Alcohol can cause direct toxic effects on the myocardium, leading to dilated cardiomyopathy characterized by systolic dysfunction, left ventricular dilation, and impaired contractility. The echocardiogram in dilated cardiomyopathy typically shows an enlarged left ventricle with reduced ejection fraction, global hypokinesis (rather than regional wall motion abnormalities), and possibly mitral regurgitation due to annular dilation. 

Other options are less likely:
- A large pericardial effusion might cause tamponade with pulsus paradoxus and distant heart sounds, not seen here.
- Left ventricular hypertrophy is more common in hypertensive heart disease or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, but this patient has no history of hypertension and presents with low blood pressure.
- Paradoxical septal motion is associated with conditions like right ventricular volume overload (e.g., atrial septal defect) or post-cardiac surgery, not fitting this history.
- Regional wall motion abnormalities are typical of coronary artery disease (e.g., myocardial infarction), but the patient did not have loss of consciousness or chest pain, and his symptoms are progressive over weeks rather than acute. His father's "heart problems" at age 78 are less suggestive of inherited early-onset coronary disease.

Given the chronic alcohol abuse and presentation, dilated cardiomyopathy is the most likely echocardiographic finding.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: Two weeks after undergoing open cholecystectomy for gangrenous cholecystitis, a 47-year-old woman comes to the clinic for removal of surgical staples. She was treated with oral vancomycin for Clostridioides difficile colitis during hospitalization. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus. Current medications are metformin and pantoprazole. She reports loose stools four times daily during the past 2 weeks. In addition to wearing a clean isolation gown, which of the following is the most appropriate precaution for the physician to take?

options.A: Washing hands thoroughly with alcohol gel
options.B: Washing hands thoroughly with soap and water
options.D: Wearing sterile gloves and preparing the staple skin line with chlorhexidine
options.E: No additional precautions are necessary 19 The abstract displayed on this page is for use with items #17–19 on the following page. Question In patients with opioid use disorder, what is the relative efficacy of buprenorphine and methadone maintenance therapy? Review scope Included studies compared buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT), >1 mg/day, with methadone maintenance therapy (MMT), ≥20 mg/day, or placebo (including 1 mg/day of buprenorphine) in patients who were dependent on heroin or other opioids. Studies of pregnant women and those assessing buprenorphine or methadone for detoxification but with no maintenance phase were excluded. Outcomes included treatment retention and urinalysis-confirmed use of morphine, cocaine, and benzodiazepines. Review methods MEDLINE and EMBASE/Excerpta Medica (2003 to Jan 2013), Cochrane Central and Cochrane Library (2013, Issue 1), PsycLIT, Current Contents, several other databases, ClinicalTrials.gov and other electronic sources of ongoing trials, conference proceedings, Library of Congress databases, national focal points for drug research, and reference lists were searched for randomized controlled trials (RCTs). Authors were consulted. 31 RCTs (n=5430), ranging in size from 40 to 736 patients, met the selection criteria. Duration of study interventions ranged from 2 weeks to 52 weeks. 20 trials compared BMT with MMT, and 11 compared BMT with placebo. Main results As shown in the table below, low-, medium-, and high-dose BMT increased treatment retention more than placebo; BMT did not differ from equivalent doses of MMT for retention or had lower retention. High-dose (standard mean difference −1.17, 95% CI −1.85 to −0.49), but not medium- or low-dose, BMT decreased morphine use more than placebo. Medium- and flexible-dose BMT and MMT did not differ for morphine use or cocaine use. BMT vs MMT and high-dose BMT vs placebo did not differ for benzodiazepine use. Buprenorphine maintenance therapy (BMT) vs methadone maintenance therapy (MMT) or placebo for treatment retention in patients with opioid use disorder Comparisons * Number of Trials (n) Weighted Retention Rates RBI (95% CI) NNT (95% CI) High -dose BMT vs placebo 5 (1001) 72% vs 40% 82% (15 to 190) 4 (3 to 7) Medium -dose BMT vs placebo 4 (887) 66% vs 38% 74% (6 to 187) 4 (2 to 34) Low-dose BMT vs placebo 5 (1131) 60% vs 40% 50% (19 to 88) 5 (4 to 10) RBR (95% CI) NNH (95% CI) High -dose BMT vs high -dose MMT 1 (134) 5.2% vs 6.6% 21% (−216 to 20) Not significant Medium -dose BMT vs medium -dose MMT 7 (780) 43% vs 48% 13% (−10 to 31) Not significant Flexible -dose BMT vs flexible -dose MMT 11 (1391) 50% vs 63% 17% (5 to 27) 8 (5 to 25) Low-dose BMT vs low -dose MMT 3 (253) 38% vs 56% 33% (13 to 48) 6 (4 to 17) CI = confidence interval; NNH = number needed to harm; NNT = number needed to treat; RBI = relative benefit increase; RBR = relative benefit reduction. *Flexible dose = dose is titrated within a broad dose range according to patient preference; low -dose BMT = 2 to 6 mg; medium -dose BMT = 7 to 15 mg; high -dose BMT = 16 mg; medium -dose MMT = 40 to 85 mg. Conclusions In patients with opioid use disorder, buprenorphine is more efficacious than placebo, but less efficacious than methadone therapy, for treatment retention. Source of funding: Australian Government Department of Health. Structured abstract based on: Mattick RP, Breen, C, Kimber J, Davoli M. Buprenorphine maintenance versus placebo or methadone maintenance for opioid dependence. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014;(2):CD002207. 24500948 20 Items #17–19 refer to the abstract displayed on the previous page.

results.question: A 17-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-hour history of progressive difficulty breathing and a 2-hour history of an itchy, red rash over his trunk, arms, and legs. He has not used new soaps, detergents, or lotions during the past week. Thirty-six hours ago, he began a 1-week course of amoxicillin for an ear infection; he has taken four doses since that time. He has no other history of serious illness and no known allergies. He takes no other medications. Pulse is 100/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 95%. He appears anxious and is in moderate respiratory distress. Examination shows an erythematous, raised, demarcated rash over the trunk and all extremities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
results.response:
Based on the clinical presentation, the patient has symptoms consistent with anaphylaxis: acute onset of progressive difficulty breathing, widespread urticarial rash (itchy, red, raised, demarcated), tachycardia (pulse 100/min), tachypnea (respirations 28/min), hypotension (BP 90/60 mm Hg), and anxiety. The symptoms began after taking amoxicillin (a common allergen), with four doses taken over 36 hours. This suggests a severe allergic reaction. Anaphylaxis is a medical emergency requiring immediate treatment.

The most appropriate next step is administration of epinephrine, which is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. Epinephrine acts quickly to reverse bronchospasm, support blood pressure, and reduce urticaria. Other options are incorrect:
- A. Albuterol might help with bronchospasm but does not address the systemic reaction (e.g., hypotension, rash).
- B. Diphenhydramine (an antihistamine) can be used adjunctively for rash and itching but is not first-line for anaphylaxis as it does not reverse hypotension or airway obstruction promptly.
- D. Complete blood count is not urgent and would not change immediate management.
- E. Observation is inappropriate given the progressive respiratory distress and hypotension.

Therefore, the correct choice is C. Administration of epinephrine.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question: A 65-year-o ld man comes to the office because of a 2-year history of progressive shortness of breath on exertion and a 6-month history of nonproductive cough. He now has shortness of breath when walking to his mailbox. He has not had fever, weight loss, or chest pain. He has not had recent sick contacts, has had no occupational exposures, and does not own any pets. He has difficult- to-control atrial fibrillation, hypertension, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and migraines. Medications are amiodarone, warfarin, lisinopril, tiotropium, and propranolol. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min and irregular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. There is no jugular venous distention. Auscultation of the lungs discloses fine crackles bilaterally, both anteriorly and posteriorly, but no egophony. The remainder of the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. An adverse effect of which of the following medications is the most likely cause of these findings?

options.E: Serum calcium and vitamin D concentrations

results.question: An 82-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-month history of increasing numbness of her feet. She has no history of serious illness. She has taken over-the-counter calcium carbonate tablets for intermittent abdominal pain during the past 40 years; she takes no other medications. She has drunk two glasses of wine with dinner nightly for 50 years. On examination, gastrocnemius deep tendon reflexes are absent. Babinski sign is absent bilaterally. Light touch to the distal lower extremities and feet produces a tingling sensation. Sensation to vibration over the great toes is severely decreased. There is a mild to moderate decrease in proprioception of the great toes. Which of the following is most likely to prevent progression of these neurologic findings?

options.B: Surgical biopsy of the oral lesion
options.C: Swabbing of the white area and sending for cytology
options.F: Vitamin B 12 (cyanocobalamin) supplementation 17 11. A 50-year-old man comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. He says he has felt well and reports no symptoms. Medical history is unremarkable. He takes no medications. He uses up to three cans of chewing tobacco weekly and occasionally sleeps with tobacco in his mouth. He says he would like to quit but cannot seem to do it on his own. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a whitish discoloration that measures approximately 1 cm in diameter on the buccal mucosa. The tongue has a brownish discoloration. Palpation of the neck discloses no lymphadenopathy. In addition to encouraging the patient to quit using chewing tobacco, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

results.correct_answer: Vitamin B 12 (cyanocobalamin) supplementation 17 11. A 50-year-old man comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. He says he has felt well and reports no symptoms. Medical history is unremarkable. He takes no medications. He uses up to three cans of chewing tobacco weekly and occasionally sleeps with tobacco in his mouth. He says he would like to quit but cannot seem to do it on his own. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a whitish discoloration that measures approximately 1 cm in diameter on the buccal mucosa. The tongue has a brownish discoloration. Palpation of the neck discloses no lymphadenopathy. In addition to encouraging the patient to quit using chewing tobacco, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
results.response:
The patient is an 82-year-old woman with a 1-month history of increasing numbness in her feet, absent gastrocnemius reflexes, decreased vibration and proprioception in the toes, and tingling with light touch. These findings suggest a peripheral neuropathy, likely sensory polyneuropathy. Her history includes long-term use of calcium carbonate tablets (which can lead to hypochlorhydria and impaired vitamin B12 absorption) and chronic alcohol use (which can cause deficiencies in B vitamins, including thiamine and B12). Vitamin B12 deficiency is a common cause of sensory neuropathy in the elderly, often due to malabsorption (e.g., pernicious anemia or hypochlorhydria from antacid use). The neurologic exam findings (impaired vibration and proprioception) are classic for B12 deficiency neuropathy. Supplementing with vitamin B12 can prevent progression and may reverse some symptoms.

Now, evaluating the options:
- A. Prescribing oral nystatin suspension: This is for fungal infections (e.g., oral thrush), not relevant here.
- B. Surgical biopsy of the oral lesion: There is no mention of an oral lesion in this patient.
- C. Swabbing of the white area and sending for cytology: Again, no oral lesion described.
- D. Observation only: This would not address the underlying deficiency and could allow progression.
- E. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) supplementation: Thiamine deficiency is associated with alcohol use and can cause neuropathy (e.g., beriberi or Wernicke-Korsakoff), but the exam findings (impaired vibration and proprioception) are more characteristic of B12 deficiency.
- F. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) supplementation: This is the most appropriate, as it directly addresses the likely deficiency caused by long-term antacid use and possibly alcohol-related malabsorption.

Therefore, vitamin B12 supplementation is most likely to prevent progression.

For the second question (which seems to be included by mistake in the output format request), the focus is on the 50-year-old man with chewing tobacco use and a whitish discoloration on the buccal mucosa. This is likely leukoplakia, a premalignant lesion. The most appropriate step is to biopsy the lesion (surgical biopsy) to rule out dysplasia or carcinoma, given the tobacco use and risk for oral cancer.

However, the primary question is about the 82-year-old woman, and the answer is F.

Belief: 0.99 (high confidence, as the clinical picture strongly points to B12 deficiency neuropathy, and supplementation is standard care).

results.question: Which of the following aspects of the study is most likely to limit confidence in the authors’ conclusions?

options.A: Exclusion of patients using methadone for acute detoxification
options.C: Inclusion of randomized controlled trials only
options.D: Inclusion of trials that compared active drug with placebo
options.E: Inclusion of trials with an intervention duration of 2 weeks
options.F: Use of urinalysis to determine treatment retention

results.correct_answer: Inclusion of trials with an intervention duration of 2 weeks
results.predicted_answer: Inclusion of trials with an intervention duration of 2 weeks
results.response:
After reviewing the question and options, the aspect most likely to limit confidence in the authors' conclusions is the inclusion of trials with an intervention duration of only 2 weeks (Option E). This is because a short duration like 2 weeks may not be sufficient to evaluate the long-term effectiveness and retention in methadone maintenance treatment for opioid dependence, which typically requires extended periods to assess true outcomes such as sustained abstinence, retention in treatment, and reduction in relapse rates. Short-term trials might not capture these critical endpoints adequately, leading to potential overestimation of benefits or underestimation of harms.

The other options are less likely to limit confidence:
- A. Exclusion of patients using methadone for acute detoxification: This is appropriate as maintenance and detoxification are different indications.
- B. Exclusion of pregnant patients: This is standard for safety reasons and to homogenize the study population.
- C. Inclusion of randomized controlled trials only: This strengthens the evidence as RCTs are high-quality.
- D. Inclusion of trials comparing active drug with placebo: This is valid for efficacy assessment.
- F. Use of urinalysis to determine treatment retention: Urinalysis is objective and commonly used; it might not be perfect but is reliable.

Thus, the short intervention duration is a significant limitation.

<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 57-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 30 minutes after he had loss of consciousness. His wife says he was making dinner when he fell on the kitchen floor. He has hypertension and his only medication is lisinopril. He has no history of operative procedures. En route, his temperature was 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse was 90/min, respirations were 10/min and labored, and blood pressure was 180/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 40% oxygen by face mask showed an oxygen saturation of 99%. On arrival, he is unresponsive. His Glasgow Coma Scale score is 3. He is intubated and mechanically ventilated. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 90/min, ventilatory rate is 10/min, and blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on an F IO2 of 0.4 shows an oxygen saturation of 98%. On examination, Doll's eye (oculocephalic) maneuver shows absent eye movements, and the corneal reflex is absent. On ice-water caloric testing, there is no nystagmus or deviation of the eyes toward the ear being irrigated. CT scan of the head shows a ruptured cerebral aneurysm. When the patient's wife is notified of the patient's condition, she says he wanted to be an organ donor but does not have an advance directive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Consult with the hospital ethics committee
options.B: Contact the organ bank for potential donation
options.C: Determine if there are any patients awaiting organ donation in the hospital
options.D: Explain that the patient is not a candidate for organ donation because he does not have an advance directive
options.E: Explain that the patient is not a candidate for organ donation because of his history of hypertension 21

results.correct_answer: Contact the organ bank for potential donation
results.predicted_answer: Contact the organ bank for potential donation
results.response:
Based on the clinical scenario, the patient has suffered a catastrophic neurological event due to a ruptured cerebral aneurysm. The examination findings indicate brainstem failure: absent oculocephalic reflex (Doll's eyes), absent corneal reflex, and absent caloric response. These are consistent with brain death. The Glasgow Coma Scale score is 3, which is the lowest possible. The patient is intubated and mechanically ventilated but has no evidence of brain function.

The wife mentions that the patient wanted to be an organ donor but does not have an advance directive. In such cases, the next appropriate step is to proceed with evaluating the patient for organ donation. The absence of an advance directive does not preclude donation; next-of-kin (in this case, the wife) can provide consent for organ donation. The history of hypertension is not an absolute contraindication for organ donation; many organs can still be donated with proper evaluation.

Therefore, the most appropriate step is to contact the organ bank to initiate the process for potential donation. This allows for formal evaluation, determination of brain death (if not already confirmed), and discussion with the family about donation.

Option A (consult ethics committee) is not necessary as there is no ethical conflict; the wife can provide consent. Option C (determine if there are patients awaiting donation) is not standard; organ allocation is managed by the organ procurement organization. Options D and E are incorrect because the absence of an advance directive or hypertension does not automatically disqualify him from donation.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 54-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination 10 days after undergoing a stereotactic brain operation to remove a small tumor. The operation was successful. During the procedure, he was under conscious sedation. The patient recalls that at one point during the operation he experienced a sudden, intense feeling of overwhelming fear. Which of the following areas of the brain was most likely stimulated at that time?
results.response:
The patient experienced a sudden, intense feeling of overwhelming fear during stimulation of the brain while under conscious sedation. The amygdala is a key structure in the brain involved in processing emotions, particularly fear and anxiety. It plays a central role in the fear response and emotional memory. Stimulation of the amygdala in humans has been shown to elicit feelings of fear, anxiety, or apprehension.

Now, evaluating the other options:
- B. Hippocampus: Primarily involved in memory formation and spatial navigation, not directly in fear emotion.
- C. Mammillary body: Part of the Papez circuit involved in memory, but not directly linked to fear emotion.
- D. Prefrontal cortex: Involved in executive functions, decision-making, and moderating social behavior, but not typically associated with sudden fear feelings.
- E. Thalamus: Acts as a relay station for sensory information, but not directly responsible for fear emotion.

Given the description, the amygdala is the most likely area stimulated to cause a sudden, intense fear feeling.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question: A 4-month-old female infant is recovering in the hospital 5 days after uncomplicated surgical repair of a ventricular septal defect via median sternotomy utilizing cardiopulmonary bypass. She was extubated on postoperative day 1 and by postoperative day 4 she was no longer receiving any inotropic infusions or intravenous drugs. She is breast-feeding well; plans are made for discharge from the hospital the next morning. However, on the next morning she develops a temperature of 38.8°C (101.8°F), cool and clammy skin, cold distal extremities, delayed capillary refill, and minimal urine output. Cardiac rhythm strip and tracing of arterial blood pressure are shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?

options.A: Add oral theophylline to the regimen
options.B: Add fluticasone by metered-dose inhaler with a spacer to the regimen
options.C: Add salmeterol by metered-dose inhaler with a spacer to the regimen

results.question: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the office because of moderate right knee pain. He is in no distress. Medical history is remarkable for hemophilia A. Examination of the right knee shows warmth and swelling; there is pain with flexion. Range of motion is limited by pain and swelling. Which of the following is the most appropriate therapy for this patient?
results.response:
The patient is a 10-year-old boy with hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) presenting with moderate knee pain, warmth, swelling, and limited range of motion, which are classic signs of a hemarthrosis (joint bleed). Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive disorder characterized by factor VIII deficiency. The standard treatment for acute bleeding episodes in hemophilia A is factor VIII replacement therapy to achieve hemostasis and prevent complications.

- Option A (ADH/vasopressin) is used in von Willebrand disease to release stored factor VIII and von Willebrand factor, but it is not sufficient for hemophilia A with an active joint bleed.
- Option B (Factor VIII concentrate) is the specific replacement therapy for hemophilia A and is the most appropriate for this acute joint bleed.
- Option C (Factor IX concentrate) is used for hemophilia B (Christmas disease), not hemophilia A.
- Option D (Ferrous sulfate) is iron supplementation used for iron deficiency anemia and not relevant for acute bleeding management.
- Option E (Fresh frozen plasma) contains all clotting factors and was historically used, but it is less efficient and not preferred now due to the risk of volume overload and availability of purified factor concentrates. Factor VIII concentrate is more specific and effective.

Thus, the most appropriate therapy is factor VIII concentrate.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>1.0</belief>

results.question: Which of the following is most likely to bias the results of this study?

options.A: Earlier diagnosis of relapse in patients receiving methadone maintenance therapy
options.B: Exclusion criteria of studies used for the review
options.C: Likelihood that more trials with positive results will be published
options.E: Variety of sample sizes of the included trials END OF SET

results.correct_answer: Likelihood that more trials with positive results will be published
results.predicted_answer: Likelihood that more trials with positive results will be published
results.response:
After analyzing the question and the options provided, the most likely source of bias in a study (particularly a systematic review or meta-analysis) is the tendency for studies with positive results to be published more often than those with negative or null results. This is known as publication bias. Publication bias can significantly skew the results of a review by overrepresenting positive findings, leading to an overestimation of the true effect size.

Let's evaluate the options:
- A. Earlier diagnosis of relapse in patients receiving methadone maintenance therapy: This could introduce detection bias in an individual trial but is not typically a primary concern for a review of multiple studies.
- B. Exclusion criteria of studies used for the review: While inappropriate exclusion criteria could introduce selection bias, this is usually addressed by following systematic review protocols.
- C. Likelihood that more trials with positive results will be published: This directly refers to publication bias, which is a major threat to the validity of systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
- D. Source of funding: Funding bias can influence study outcomes, but it is often considered a subset of publication bias or other biases, and may not be the most direct or common issue in reviews.
- E. Variety of sample sizes of the included trials: This might affect the weight of studies in a meta-analysis but is not inherently biasing; it is accounted for with statistical methods.

Given that the question is about a study (likely a review) and the options pertain to biases that could affect its results, option C (publication bias) is the most pervasive and likely to bias the overall findings.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: Two days after admission to the hospital for treatment of a fractured femur and closed head injury sustained in a motor vehicle collision, an 8-year-old girl has a brief, generalized tonic-clonic seizure. CT scan of the head on admission disclosed no abnormalities. She has no history of serious illness. Oral acetaminophen was initiated for pain, and the patient underwent open reduction and internal fixation of the fracture. She receives no routine medications. Today, she appears drowsy but responds to questions appropriately. She is at the 55th percentile for height, weight, and BMI. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 92/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 106/64 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 95%. results.question: A 36-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 39 weeks’ gestation is admitted to the hospital in labor. She reports painful contractions that occur every 2 to 3 minutes and last 60 seconds. She has not had vaginal bleeding. She has no history of serious illness, and pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 84/min and regular, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. The abdomen is nontender and consistent in size with a 39-week gestation. External fetal monitoring shows a heart rate of 150/min with moderate variability, several spontaneous accelerations, and no decelerations. An external tocometer shows regular uterine contractions every 2 to 3 minutes. The cervix is 6 cm dilated and 100% effaced; the vertex is at 0 station. Ultrasonography shows the fetus in a cephalic presentation. The membranes are artificially ruptured, yielding copious clear fluid. Thirty minutes later, the cervix is 9 cm dilated and 100% effaced; the vertex is at +1 station. A fetal heart tracing is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Advising the patient to begin pushing

results.response:
Based on the clinical scenario, the patient is in active labor at 39 weeks' gestation with normal vital signs and previously reassuring fetal status (moderate variability, accelerations, no decelerations). After artificial rupture of membranes (which yielded clear fluid), the cervix has progressed rapidly from 6 cm to 9 cm dilation in 30 minutes, and the vertex is now at +1 station. This indicates rapid progress in the active phase of labor without any signs of fetal distress initially.

However, the question mentions "a fetal heart tracing is shown" but does not provide the actual tracing. In the context of USMLE questions, when a tracing is referenced but not displayed, it often implies there is an abnormality that must be inferred from the options. The options include interventions for potential fetal distress (e.g., amnioinfusion for variable decelerations suggestive of cord compression, forceps delivery or cesarean for urgent delivery), as well as expectant management or advising pushing.

Given the rapid progress (from 6 cm to 9 cm in 30 minutes) and the fact that the patient is almost fully dilated (9 cm), the most likely abnormality in the tracing is recurrent variable decelerations due to cord compression after rupture of membranes. This is a common scenario after amniotomy, especially with copious clear fluid (which might indicate oligohydramnios post-rupture). Amnioinfusion is a standard intervention for recurrent variable decelerations to relieve cord compression by infusing fluid into the uterine cavity.

The other options are less appropriate:
- Advising pushing (A) is premature at 9 cm without full dilation.
- Forceps-assisted delivery (D) or cesarean (E) would be too aggressive without first attempting simpler measures like amnioinfusion, especially since there was no prior non-reassuring status.
- Expectant management (C) might not be sufficient if there are significant decelerations.

Therefore, amnioinfusion (B) is the most appropriate next step to address the likely cord compression indicated by the tracing.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 72-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 3-week history of visual changes in his right eye. During this period, he has noticed that the lines of his daily crossword puzzle look curved, and the blinds in his apartment appear wavy. He wears magnifying lenses for reading. He has not had pain in his eye or photophobia. He has no history of trauma to the area. On examination, visual acuity is 20/200 in the right eye and 20/100 in the left eye. Both pupils are round and reactive to light. Palpation of both globes through closed eyelids shows no abnormalities. There is normal red reflex bilaterally. The lenses appear clear. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
results.response:
Based on the clinical presentation, the most likely diagnosis is macular degeneration. The patient is a 72-year-old man with a 3-week history of visual changes characterized by metamorphopsia (distortion of straight lines, such as wavy blinds and curved crossword lines), which is a classic symptom of macular degeneration. This is often due to fluid accumulation or distortion in the macula. His age is a key risk factor for age-related macular degeneration. The absence of pain, photophobia, or trauma, along with clear lenses and normal red reflex, rules out cataracts (which typically cause gradual blurring without distortion). Central retinal artery occlusion usually presents with sudden, painless vision loss, not gradual distortion. Closed-angle glaucoma is associated with pain, redness, and photophobia. Temporal arteritis can cause vision loss but is often accompanied by systemic symptoms like headache, jaw claudication, and tenderness, which are not mentioned here. The visual acuity is reduced more in the right eye (20/200) than the left (20/100), consistent with asymmetric macular involvement.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 28-year-ol d man comes to the clinic because of a 2-day history of a red, severely painful lesion on his left forearm. He says that he felt stinging pain as he put his shirt on in the dark 2 days ago; the pain was so severe he immediately threw down the shirt and saw a spider running across the floor. He returned yesterday from a 1-week trip to the mountains in the southeastern United States. He has type 1 diabetes mellitus treated with lisinopril and insulin. He does not drink alcoholic beverages. He is sexually active with two male partners and uses condoms inconsistently. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the lesion is shown. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following physical findings is most likely to appear during the next 24 hours?

options.A: Areas of eschar at the center of the wound site
options.B: Gangrene of the distal aspect of the left extremity
options.D: Palpable purpuric lesions across the trunk and upper extremities
options.E: Rapidly spreading erythema around the wound site
options.F: Tender, erythematous streaks up to the epitrochlear nodes

results.correct_answer: Areas of eschar at the center of the wound site
results.predicted_answer: Areas of eschar at the center of the wound site
results.response:
After analyzing the case, the patient likely experienced a bite from a brown recluse spider (Loxosceles reclusa), which is common in the southeastern United States. The initial stinging pain and the sighting of a spider support this. The brown recluse bite often starts with severe pain and redness, and within 24-72 hours, it can develop central ischemia leading to a characteristic eschar (necrotic tissue) at the center of the wound due to the venom's hemolytic and cytotoxic effects.

Reviewing the options:
- A. Areas of eschar at the center of the wound site: This is highly characteristic of a brown recluse spider bite and typically appears within the next 24-48 hours.
- B. Gangrene of the distal aspect: This is less likely to occur so rapidly and is not typical; eschar is localized.
- C. Generalized upper extremity edema: This is not typical; edema is usually localized.
- D. Palpable purpuric lesions: This suggests vasculitis (e.g., meningococcemia) but doesn't fit the spider bite scenario.
- E. Rapidly spreading erythema: This is more indicative of cellulitis or erysipelas, which might occur later but not as specifically as eschar.
- F. Tender, erythematous streaks: This suggests lymphangitis (e.g., from bacterial infection), which isn't the primary finding here.

The most specific and likely finding within the next 24 hours is the development of eschar at the bite site.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic for follow-up examination. She has opioid use disorder and currently takes methadone 40 mg daily as maintenance therapy. She says the methadone is causing mild sedation that is interfering with her job. She would like to discontinue methadone maintenance therapy but is concerned about heroin relapse. She says she also takes a friend’s valium when she is feeling “stressed out.” The physician discusses alternative maintenance therapy strategies with the patient. The physician discusses the pros and cons of medium- or high-dose buprenorphine maintenance therapy compared with medium- or high-dose methadone therapy with the patient. Based on this abstract, which of the following is the most appropriate information for the physician to convey to the patient?

options.A: Buprenorphine is 13% and 21% less effective than methadone at medium and high doses, respectively
options.B: Buprenorphine is a better maintenance strategy if the patient is also regularly using benzodiazepines
options.C: Methadone is statistically more likely to result in treatment retention
options.D: Neither drug is more effective than placebo
options.E: There is an unclear difference in efficacy between the two drugs at these doses

results.correct_answer: There is an unclear difference in efficacy between the two drugs at these doses
results.predicted_answer: Buprenorphine is a better maintenance strategy if the patient is also regularly using benzodiazepines
results.response:
Based on the question, the patient is concerned about methadone causing sedation and is intermittently using benzodiazepines (valium). The physician is discussing the pros and cons of buprenorphine versus methadone maintenance therapy. 

Key points to consider:
- Buprenorphine is a partial agonist with a ceiling effect, which may lead to less sedation and lower risk of respiratory depression compared to methadone (a full agonist). 
- Concurrent use of benzodiazepines with opioids increases the risk of respiratory depression and overdose. However, buprenorphine's ceiling effect might make it safer in this context compared to methadone, which has no ceiling effect and higher risk when combined with benzodiazepines.
- Evidence shows that both buprenorphine and methadone are effective for opioid maintenance therapy, but methadone is generally associated with higher retention rates. However, the patient's specific situation (sedation issues and benzodiazepine use) may favor buprenorphine.
- Option A states buprenorphine is less effective than methadone at medium and high doses, which is supported by some studies (e.g., methadone has better retention rates), but this might not be the most critical factor given the patient's concerns.
- Option B claims buprenorphine is better if regularly using benzodiazepines. While buprenorphine is generally safer with benzodiazepines due to its ceiling effect, it is still dangerous and not recommended. However, compared to methadone, it has a lower risk profile in combination with benzodiazepines.
- Option C says methadone is statistically more likely to result in treatment retention, which is true based on meta-analyses, but again, may not address the sedation and safety concerns.
- Option D is incorrect as both drugs are more effective than placebo.
- Option E is vague and not accurate; there is evidence showing differences in efficacy and safety.

The most appropriate information to convey in this context is regarding the safety of buprenorphine with benzodiazepine use. Although any opioid with benzodiazepines is risky, buprenorphine is associated with a lower risk of fatal overdose compared to methadone when combined with benzodiazepines. Therefore, for a patient intermittently using benzodiazepines, buprenorphine might be a safer option.

Thus, option B is the most appropriate information to convey.

Belief: 0.85 (high confidence, but noting that any benzodiazepine use with opioids is dangerous and requires caution; however, buprenorphine is relatively safer compared to methadone in this scenario).

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

results.question: An 87-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 4-day history of moderate abdominal cramps, nausea, and vomiting. He has not had a bowel movement or passed gas in the past 24 hours. He has colon cancer metastatic to the lungs, liver, and peritoneum. Eighteen months ago, he underwent a low anterior resection for T3N2 rectal cancer. Six months ago, exploratory laparotomy showed diffuse carcinomatosis and bowel obstruction, and an ileotransverse bypass was performed. The patient and his family were informed of the findings and advised that he would not be a candidate for a laparotomy in the future because there would be no surgical options to treat another obstruction. The patient has no other history of serious illness. Medications are 5-fluorouracil and bevacizumab. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. The abdomen is distended and mildly tender to palpation. There are no peritoneal signs. CT scan of the abdomen shows a mid-jejunal small-bowel obstruction, multiple intraperitoneal tumor deposits, and ascites. The physician recommends insertion of nasogastric and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tubes. The patient and his family request that the patient undergo a laparotomy and intestinal bypass. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Abide by the wishes of the patient and his family
options.C: Explain the futility of the operation to the patient and his family
options.D: Offer to transfer the patient to another facility

results.correct_answer: Explain the futility of the operation to the patient and his family
results.predicted_answer: Explain the futility of the operation to the patient and his family
results.response:
The patient has advanced metastatic colon cancer with diffuse carcinomatosis and a history of prior surgery and bypass for obstruction. The current presentation is a recurrent small-bowel obstruction due to progressive disease. The patient and family are requesting laparotomy and intestinal bypass, but the physician had previously advised that no surgical options exist for future obstructions due to the lack of surgical solutions and high risks. Given the patient's poor prognosis, extensive disease, and prior explicit discussion about futility of further surgery, performing another laparotomy would not provide benefit and could cause harm, including increased morbidity, mortality, and prolonged suffering without resolving the underlying issue.

The most appropriate step is to explain the futility of the operation, focusing on compassionate communication about the goals of care, symptom management (e.g., with nasogastric decompression, gastrostomy for comfort), and transitioning to palliative approaches. This aligns with ethical principles of avoiding non-beneficial treatments while respecting patient autonomy through clear information and shared decision-making.

Option A (abiding by wishes) is inappropriate as surgery is futile and harmful. Option B (altering chemotherapy) is not urgent and won't relieve obstruction. Option D (transfer) is not necessary as care is appropriate. Option E (radiation) is not standard for obstruction in this context and unlikely to help.

Thus, explaining futility (option C) is correct.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: Today, his blood pressure is 138/87 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. In addition to recommending weight loss, which of the following is the most appropriate next step to prevent cardiovascular morbidity in this patient?
results.correct_answer: Thiazide diuretic therapy 24 28. A 39-year-old man comes to the office because of a 6-week history of mild fatigue and increased thirst and urination. During this time, he also has had a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. He is otherwise asymptomatic. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 61 kg (135 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 118/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Serum Na+ 135 mEq/L K+ 4.2 mEq/L Cl− 100 mEq/L Urea nitrogen 25 mg/dL Glucose 578 mg/dL Blood Hemoglobin A 1c 10.7% In addition to recommending dietary modification, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
results.response:
Based on the question, the patient has a blood pressure of 138/87 mm Hg, which is classified as stage 1 hypertension according to ACC/AHA guidelines. The question mentions "in addition to recommending weight loss," and the options are all diabetes medications. This implies that the patient likely has type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), as weight loss is a common recommendation for both hypertension and T2DM. The goal is to prevent cardiovascular morbidity.

Among the options:
- Glyburide (A) is a sulfonylurea that lowers blood glucose but is associated with hypoglycemia and weight gain, and it does not have cardiovascular benefits.
- Insulin therapy (B) effectively lowers glucose but can cause weight gain and hypoglycemia, and it has not been shown to reduce cardiovascular events specifically.
- Metformin therapy (C) is a first-line treatment for T2DM. It promotes weight loss, improves insulin sensitivity, and has been shown to reduce cardiovascular events and mortality in patients with T2DM.
- Pioglitazone (D) is a thiazolidinedione that improves insulin sensitivity but can cause weight gain, fluid retention, heart failure, and has mixed cardiovascular outcomes (though it may reduce stroke and MI in some studies).
- Sitagliptin (E) is a DPP-4 inhibitor that is weight-neutral but has not demonstrated cardiovascular benefits in large trials.

Metformin is the most appropriate choice because it is recommended as first-line therapy for T2DM, aids in weight loss, and has proven cardiovascular benefits, including reducing myocardial infarction and cardiovascular mortality. This aligns with the goal of preventing cardiovascular morbidity.

Given the strong evidence for metformin in cardiovascular risk reduction in T2DM patients, I am highly confident in this answer.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 27-year-old w oman comes to the office because of a 3-week history of nasal congestion, cough productive of sputum, intermittent moderate headache, and intermittent moderate pain over her cheeks. She has had similar symptoms three to four times yearly during the past 5 years. The symptoms last 2 to 8 weeks and generally resolve spontaneously. She has had two episodes of bacterial pneumonia during the past 5 years; both episodes resolved with antibiotic therapy. Her only current medication is acetaminophen as needed for headache. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum study results show decreased IgA, IgG, and IgM concentrations. Antibiotic therapy is begun, and the patient’s condition improves. Which of the following is most likely to decrease the likelihood of recurrent infection in this patient?
results.correct_answer: Monthly immune globulin replacement therapy 22 23. A 37-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-year history of mild to moderate fatigue. The fatigue waxes and wanes without relation to activity. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He drinks three glasses of whiskey weekly. He has had two lifetime female sexual partners; he uses condoms consistently. He immigrated to the United States from China 15 years ago. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Serum Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL ALT 85 U/L AST 60 U/L HBsAg positive Anti-HCV negative Anti-HBs negative IgG anti -HBc positive Blood Hematocrit 35% Hemoglobin 12 g/dL MCV 82 µm3 Without treatment of his current condition, this patient is most likely to develop which of the following?
results.response:
The patient has a history of recurrent sinopulmonary infections, bacterial pneumonia, and low levels of all immunoglobulins (IgA, IgG, IgM), which suggests a primary immunodeficiency disorder, likely common variable immunodeficiency (CVID). CVID is characterized by hypogammaglobulinemia and impaired antibody production, leading to recurrent infections. The question asks which option is most likely to decrease the likelihood of recurrent infection. The key management for CVID is immunoglobulin replacement therapy, which reduces the frequency and severity of infections. However, the options provided are complications or associated conditions rather than treatments.

Reviewing the options:
- A. Amyloidosis: This is a complication of chronic inflammatory conditions, including recurrent infections, but it does not reduce infections; it may occur as a consequence.
- B. Essential mixed cryoglobulinemia: This is associated with chronic infections (like hepatitis C) or autoimmune diseases, but not directly with reducing infections.
- C. Hepatocellular carcinoma: This is a complication of chronic liver disease and not related to reducing infections.
- D. Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis: This can be associated with chronic infections (e.g., hepatitis C) or complement deficiencies, but it does not reduce infections.
- E. Polyarteritis nodosa: This is a vasculitis that can be associated with infections (e.g., hepatitis B), but it does not reduce infections.
- F. Sjögren syndrome: This is an autoimmune disorder that can be associated with CVID. Interestingly, some patients with CVID may develop autoimmune phenomena like Sjögren syndrome. However, the development of Sjögren syndrome does not reduce infections; it might complicate the clinical course.

The thyroid bruit indicates increased blood flow, and the extrathyroidal manifestations like ophthalmopathy and dermopathy are specific to Graves'.

Now, looking at the options provided:

D. Smoking cessation – Smoking is a risk factor for Graves' disease and can worsen ophthalmopathy, so cessation is important but not the diagnosis.
E. Thyrotoxicosis – This is the correct diagnosis, specifically Graves' disease given the full clinical picture.

The second part about the 28-year-old male with hematochezia, iron deficiency anemia, loose bowel movements, no abdominal pain, smoking history, and colonoscopy showing coalesced ulcers in the ileum with normal mucosa in between is classic for Crohn's disease (which can affect the terminal ileum). The most appropriate recommendation for him is smoking cessation, as smoking exacerbates Crohn's disease and increases the risk of flares and complications.

Thus, for the initial question, the most likely diagnosis is thyrotoxicosis (Graves' disease).

results.question: A 19-year-old man is brought to the emergency department (ED) by ambulance 15 minutes after he was stabbed in the chest during a fight. The stab wound is located medially to the left nipple. At the scene, pulse was 105/min, respirations were 18/min, and blood pressure was 120/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air showed an oxygen saturation of 96%. Two large-bore intravenous catheters were placed en route to the ED, and lactated Ringer solution was initiated. On arrival in the trauma bay, the patient develops a pulse of 130/min and blood pressure of 80/40 mm Hg. There is jugular venous distention. Results of focused assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST) are most likely to lead to which of the following next steps for this patient?

options.A: Ask the patient if she recalls signing the consent form and, if so, proceed with the operation
options.B: Ask the patient to sign a new consent form and proceed with the operation
options.C: Do not proceed, and remove the patient from the operating room
options.D: Obtain verbal consent from the patient's husband before proceeding with the operation
options.E: Proceed with the operative procedure as planned

results.predicted_answer: Proceed with the operative procedure as planned
results.response:
After analyzing the clinical scenario, the patient has a stab wound medial to the left nipple, which is a classic location for a cardiac injury (potentially the right ventricle). The rapid deterioration with hypotension (BP 80/40), tachycardia (pulse 130/min), and jugular venous distention (JVD) strongly suggests cardiac tamponade. The FAST exam is highly sensitive for detecting pericardial fluid and tamponade in trauma. A positive FAST in this context would confirm tamponade, which is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate pericardiocentesis or, more definitively, a thoracotomy/pericardial window. There is no time for delays like consent discussions; this is an emergency where implied consent applies.

Now, evaluating the options:
- A, B, D: These involve obtaining consent (either from the patient or husband), which is inappropriate and dangerous in this emergency. The patient is unstable and likely cannot give valid consent due to his condition.
- C: Not proceeding and removing the patient from the OR would be fatal, as tamponade requires immediate intervention.
- E: Proceeding with the operative procedure as planned is correct. The team should perform an emergency thoracotomy or pericardial window without delay under implied consent.

Thus, the correct next step is to proceed with the operation.

<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question: A 17-year-old girl comes to the office because of a 3-month history of daily moderate epigastric pain that is more severe after meals. Three weeks ago, she had watery diarrhea for 2 days, which resolved spontaneously. She has not had vomiting. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination, including abdominal examination, shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
results.response:
After analyzing the case, the patient is a 17-year-old girl with a 3-month history of daily moderate epigastric pain that worsens after meals, and a recent brief episode of watery diarrhea. This presentation is highly suggestive of **eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)**, which commonly presents with epigastric pain, dysphagia, and food impaction, and is often triggered or worsened by meals. EoE is also associated with other atopic conditions, and diarrhea could be related to a concurrent allergic gastroenteritis.

Among the options:
- **A. Albuterol therapy** is for asthma, not relevant here.
- **B. Bronchoscopy** is for evaluating the airways, not the GI tract.
- **C. CT scan of the chest** might not be specific for GI mucosal issues.
- **D. Intubation** is not diagnostic; it's a procedure for airway management.
- **E. Methylprednisolone therapy** is a corticosteroid. In EoE, a **steroid challenge** (like oral methylprednisolone) is sometimes used diagnostically because symptoms often improve significantly with steroids, supporting the diagnosis. While endoscopy with biopsy is the gold standard, it is not listed. Among the given choices, methylprednisolone therapy is the most likely to confirm the diagnosis by demonstrating clinical improvement.

Thus, the correct option is E.

<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

results.question: Pulse is 104/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Examination shows dry mucous membranes. ECG shows a second-degree atrioventricular block. Which of the following is most likely responsible for this patient's confusion?
results.response:
After analyzing the patient's presentation, the key findings are tachycardia (pulse 104/min), mild hypertension (BP 140/90 mm Hg), dry mucous membranes (indicating dehydration), second-degree AV block on ECG, and confusion. These are classic signs of lithium toxicity.

Lithium is a mood stabilizer known to cause a variety of toxic effects. Its toxicity often presents with:
- Neurological symptoms: confusion, tremor, ataxia.
- Cardiac manifestations: various arrhythmias, including sinus node dysfunction and AV block (as seen here).
- Renal effects: nephrogenic diabetes insipidus leading to polyuria, polydipsia, and dehydration (dry mucous membranes).
- Gastrointestinal symptoms: nausea, vomiting.

The ECG finding of a second-degree AV block is particularly characteristic of lithium toxicity, as lithium can depress cardiac conduction. The confusion aligns with its neurotoxic effects, and the dry mucous membranes suggest dehydration, which is a risk factor for lithium toxicity as it reduces renal clearance of the drug.

Now, evaluating the other options:
- **A. Acetaminophen**: Toxicity causes hepatic necrosis, not AV block or confusion in this context.
- **B. Bupropion**: Associated with seizures and tachycardia, but not AV block or dehydration.
- **D. Risperidone**: An antipsychotic that can cause sedation or extrapyramidal symptoms, but not typically AV block or dehydration.
- **E. Topiramate**: An antiepileptic that can cause metabolic acidosis, glaucoma, or nephrolithiasis, but not AV block.

Therefore, lithium is the most likely cause.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question: A grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur is heard best at the apex. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Blood Hematocrit 35% WBC 13,000/mm3 Platelet count 357,000/mm3 PTT 38 sec PT 57 sec (INR=5.2) Which of the following actions is most likely to have prevented the near miss in this patient?

options.D: Venous duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity

results.response:
The patient has an elevated PT/INR (57 sec, INR=5.2), indicating significant anticoagulation, which increases the risk of bleeding. The holosystolic murmur at the apex suggests mitral regurgitation, but this is likely incidental and not the primary issue. The key finding is the over-anticoagulation, which could be due to a drug interaction, especially since the patient is probably on warfarin (given the PT/INR monitoring). Warfarin has numerous drug interactions that can potentiate its effect and lead to dangerously high INR levels, increasing bleeding risk. Reviewing for potential drug-drug interactions could identify and prevent such over-anticoagulation, avoiding a near miss (e.g., a bleeding event).

- Option A (Review for potential drug-drug interaction) is correct as it addresses the cause of the elevated INR.
- Dietary counseling (B) might be relevant for warfarin stability but is less direct and not the most immediate preventive measure.
- Echocardiography (C) would assess the murmur but doesn't address the anticoagulation issue.
- Venous duplex ultrasonography (D) might be for DVT but is unrelated.
- Discontinuation of allopurinol (E) might be considered if allopurinol is interacting (it can potentiate warfarin), but it is specific to one possible interaction, whereas a broad review (A) is more comprehensive and standard.

Thus, the most likely action to prevent the near miss is reviewing drug interactions.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the office for evaluation of short stature. Since birth, she has been below the 3rd percentile for height and at the 3rd percentile for weight. She has never had a menstrual period. There is no family history of short stature, but her maternal uncle did not begin puberty until the age of 16 years. Her current height is consistent with that of a 9½-year-old girl. Pulse is 82/min and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Sexual maturity rating is stage 1 for breast development and stage 2 for pubic hair development. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.A: Complete blood count and serum chemistry panel
options.B: Measurement of urine free cortisol concentration
options.C: X-rays of the left hand and wrist
options.D: X-rays of the long bones and spine

results.correct_answer: X-rays of the left hand and wrist
results.predicted_answer: X-rays of the left hand and wrist
results.response:
After analyzing the case, the most appropriate next step is to obtain X-rays of the left hand and wrist for bone age assessment.

The patient is a 13-year-old girl with significant short stature (height at the level of a 9.5-year-old), pre-pubertal status (no menarche, SMR stage 1 breasts), and a family history of delayed puberty (maternal uncle). Her growth has been consistently below the 3rd percentile since birth. This pattern suggests a possible constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP), which is a common cause of short stature and delayed puberty. The first and most crucial diagnostic step in evaluating short stature in a child is to determine the bone age, which is done by an X-ray of the left hand and wrist. This helps to assess skeletal maturity and compare it to chronological age. A delayed bone age is characteristic of CDGP and would support this diagnosis, guiding further management.

The other options are less appropriate:
- A (CBC and chemistry panel) might be part of a general workup but is not specific for evaluating short stature or delayed puberty.
- B (Urine free cortisol) is used to screen for Cushing's syndrome, which can cause short stature, but it is not the first step; patients with Cushing's often have weight gain and hypertension, which are absent here.
- D (X-rays of long bones and spine) might be used to look for skeletal dysplasias, but these are usually evident earlier and associated with disproportion, which isn't mentioned.
- E (X-ray of the skull) could assess the sella turcica for pituitary tumors, but this is unlikely without headaches or visual symptoms, and bone age should come first.

Therefore, option C is the best next step.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 20-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-year history of a lump on her left ear that appeared 2 months after she had the ear pierced. Physical examination shows the findings in the photograph. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient?
results.response:
Based on the description of a lump on the ear that appeared after piercing, this is highly suggestive of a keloid. Keloids are benign fibrous growths that result from an overgrowth of scar tissue. Common treatments include corticosteroid injections, which are often first-line due to their ability to reduce inflammation and shrink the keloid.

Reviewing the options:
- A. Corticosteroid injection: This is a standard initial treatment for keloids, as it helps flatten and soften the scar.
- B. Cryotherapy: Can be used but is often combined with corticosteroids and is not typically the first choice alone.
- C. Laser ablation: Used for some keloids but usually not initial; more for refractory cases.
- D. Needle aspiration: Not appropriate for solid keloids; used for fluid-filled lesions like cysts.
- E. Wide excision: This can actually increase the risk of recurrence if not combined with other therapies like corticosteroids or radiation.

For a small keloid on the ear after piercing, intralesional corticosteroid injection is the most appropriate initial treatment to reduce size and symptoms.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: After intravenous administration of fluids and nasogastric tube lavage, the patient undergoes esophagogastroduodenoscopy. A photograph of the endoscopic findings is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
results.response:
After reviewing the clinical scenario, the patient has received initial stabilization with IV fluids and nasogastric lavage, which are common steps for upper GI bleeding. The esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) was performed, and a photograph is provided (though not visible here). The options include various management strategies.

Given that the patient is undergoing EGD for suspected upper GI bleeding (as indicated by the resuscitation and lavage), the most common cause is peptic ulcer disease or variceal bleeding. However, the options include therapies targeting different pathologies:

- Octreotide is used for variceal bleeding.
- Omeprazole is standard for peptic ulcer disease.
- Tyrosine kinase inhibitors are for neoplasms like GIST.
- Endoscopic hemostatic therapy (e.g., clipping, coagulation) is applied for active bleeding.
- Endoscopic biopsy or resection would be for suspicious lesions, likely neoplasms.

Without the photograph, the context suggests that the EGD is for diagnosing and treating the source of bleeding. Since the patient required resuscitation, active bleeding is probable. The standard of care during EGD for active bleeding is to apply hemostatic therapy (e.g., thermal coagulation, clips) if a bleeding vessel is identified. This is immediate and effective.

Option D, "Endoscopic hemostatic therapy," is the most appropriate next step during the same endoscopic procedure if active bleeding is visualized. This aligns with guidelines for managing acute GI hemorrhage, where endoscopic intervention is first-line.

Belief: 0.95 (high confidence, as this is a well-established practice; however, slight uncertainty due to the unseen photograph which might show a specific lesion altering management).

results.response:
After reviewing the case, I see there are two separate questions presented. The first question is about a 77-year-old woman with a vulvar lesion, and the second is about a 68-year-old man with low back pain. However, the options listed (A through F) seem to be for the second question, as they include diagnoses like abdominal aortic aneurysm, osteoporosis, etc., and option F is "Biopsy of the vulva," which is clearly for the first patient.

Given the output format requires selecting one letter and providing a belief, I need to focus on the correct question. The text says "ANSWER THE MULTIPLE-CHOICE QUESTION," and the last part describes the man with back pain, so I believe the intended question is for the 68-year-old man.

For the 68-year-old man:
- He has a 3-month history of low back pain radiating to both legs.
- Pain is triggered by standing or walking and relieved by sitting or lying down.
- It did not respond to NSAIDs but improves with rest.
- He has a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a 40-pack-year smoking history.
- Physical exam shows delayed expiration (suggestive of COPD/emphysema from smoking), a systolic murmur (possibly aortic stenosis), trace ankle edema, but normal ankle-brachial index (ruling out peripheral artery disease).
- Neurologic and musculoskeletal exams are normal.

This presentation is classic for neurogenic claudication due to lumbar spinal stenosis. The pain is exacerbated by standing and walking (which extends the spine and narrows the canal) and relieved by sitting or lying down (which flexes the spine and opens the canal). The normal neurologic exam is common early on, and the smoking history is a risk factor for degenerative changes.

Now, evaluating the options:
A. Abdominal aortic aneurysm – can cause back pain but is often constant, not positional, and not typically relieved by sitting.
B. Osteoporosis – might cause compression fractures, but pain is usually not radicular or positional in that way.
C. Peripheral neuropathy – usually symmetric, burning, not positional.
D. Spinal stenosis – fits perfectly with neurogenic claudication.
E. Vascular claudication – pain is due to ischemia, triggered by exercise, relieved by rest (not position), and often associated with diminished pulses or low ABI; here ABI is 1.0, ruling it out.
F. Biopsy of the vulva – clearly for the first patient, not relevant.

Thus, the most likely diagnosis is spinal stenosis.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: In addition to intravenous administration of fluids and nasogastric tube lavage, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
results.response:
After reviewing the question and options, the most appropriate next step in management for a patient with suspected upper GI bleeding who has already received IV fluids and nasogastric tube lavage is esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD). This is because EGD is the gold standard for diagnosing and potentially treating the source of upper GI bleeding (such as varices, ulcers, or erosions) and should be performed urgently in unstable patients. 

The other options are less appropriate:
- Abdominal CT scan (A) might be useful for other abdominal issues but not first-line for acute GI bleed.
- Octreotide scan (B) is for neuroendocrine tumors, not acute management.
- Technetium 99m scan (C) is for Meckel's diverticulum or slow bleeds, not urgent.
- Colonoscopy (D) is for lower GI bleeding, not upper.
- Mesenteric angiography (F) is typically reserved for cases where endoscopy fails or for lower GI bleeds.

Thus, EGD is the correct choice.

<answer>E</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 39-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-hour history of moderate pain and swelling of his right knee and ankle. His symptoms began after he scaled a wall during an obstacle course. He landed awkwardly on his right leg and rolled his right ankle; his knee buckled. He limped to the finish line. He underwent reconstruction of a torn right anterior cruciate ligament 15 years ago. He and his boyfriend are engaged to be married. He feels safe at home. He does not appear to be in distress. Examination of the right knee shows mild tenderness to palpation of the midsagittal joint line. Adduction and abduction stress testing is stable at 0 and 30 degrees of flexion. Range of motion is full. Lachman test is negative. There is moderate tenderness to palpation of the distal aspect of the fibula. There are ecchymoses over the anterolateral aspect of the right ankle. The ankle is swollen. There is no tenderness to palpation of the medial aspect of the ankle. Range of motion of the ankle is limited to 5 degrees of dorsiflexion and 30 degrees of plantar flexion because of pain. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis? Knee Ankle

options.D: No diagnostic testing indicated no diagnostic testing indicated

results.response:
After analyzing the case, the patient has a history of an awkward landing with rolling of the ankle and knee buckling, leading to pain and swelling in the right knee and ankle. The knee exam shows mild tenderness but stable stress tests, negative Lachman (indicating the ACL graft is intact), and full range of motion. There is no evidence of significant ligamentous injury or instability in the knee. The ankle exam shows moderate tenderness over the distal fibula, ecchymoses anterolaterally, swelling, and limited range of motion due to pain, with no medial tenderness. This suggests a likely lateral ankle injury, such as a sprain or possible fracture.

Given the mechanism (inversion injury) and findings (tenderness over fibula, ecchymoses, swelling), an Ottawa Ankle Rule assessment is appropriate. The Ottawa rules recommend ankle X-rays if there is pain in the malleolar zone plus any of: bone tenderness at the posterior edge or tip of the lateral malleolus (distal fibula), inability to bear weight immediately after injury, or bone tenderness at the posterior edge or tip of the medial malleolus. Here, there is tenderness over the distal fibula (lateral malleolus) and inability to bear weight (he limped), so X-ray is indicated for the ankle to rule out a fracture.

For the knee, despite the history of ACL reconstruction, the exam is stable with no signs of acute injury (negative Lachman, stable stress tests, full ROM). There is no indication for knee X-ray based on the Ottawa Knee Rules, which require X-ray only if: age 55 or older, isolated patellar tenderness, tenderness at head of fibula, inability to flex to 90°, or inability to bear weight immediately. None of these are present here.

Therefore, the most appropriate next step is no diagnostic testing for the knee and X-ray for the ankle.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: Examination shows a 1 × 2-cm, erythematous, soft lesion over the posterior plantar aspect of the left foot. Sensation to pinprick and touch is decreased over the ankles and feet. Hemoglobin A 1c is 7%. Which of the following findings are most likely to be present in this patient? Glucose Insulin Insulin Receptor Responsiveness
results.correct_answer: Normal normal normal 33 49. A 57-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of fever, chills, and sore throat. She is on day 6 of a 7-day course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for treatment of cystitis. She has been taking lisinopril nightly for 2 years for treatment of hypertension. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no other medications. Temperature is 38.6°C (101.4°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg. Examination shows erythema of the pharynx but no exudates. No other abnormalities are noted. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Blood Hematocrit 38% WBC 2200/mm3 Neutrophils, segmented 16% Neutrophils, bands 4% Lymphocytes 64% Monocytes 16% Platelet count 225,000/mm3 Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
results.response:
The patient presents with a foot lesion that appears to be a neuropathic ulcer, given the decreased sensation over the ankles and feet (indicating peripheral neuropathy) and an elevated hemoglobin A1c of 7% (indicating poorly controlled diabetes mellitus). Peripheral neuropathy and foot ulcers are common complications of diabetes due to microvascular damage and impaired wound healing. 

Now, evaluating the options:
- A. Acute mononucleosis: Typically presents with fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, and fatigue; not associated with neuropathic ulcers or diabetes.
- B. Acute myelogenous leukemia: Presents with symptoms like fatigue, bleeding, infections, and cytopenias; not directly related to diabetic complications.
- C. Adverse effect of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole: This drug can cause side effects like rash, hyperkalemia, or hematologic issues, but not typically neuropathic ulcers.
- D. Allergic reaction to lisinopril: Can cause angioedema or cough, but not neuropathic ulcers.
- E. Myelofibrosis: A myeloproliferative disorder leading to bone marrow fibrosis, splenomegaly, and cytopenias; not associated with diabetes or foot ulcers.
- F. Sepsis syndrome: Involves systemic inflammation and infection, which could complicate a foot ulcer but is not the primary finding; the ulcer itself is more likely due to diabetes.

The most likely finding is that the foot lesion is a diabetic neuropathic ulcer, which is a direct complication of diabetes. None of the options directly state "diabetic ulcer," but among the choices, sepsis syndrome (F) could be present if the ulcer becomes infected, which is common in diabetic foot ulcers due to impaired immunity and circulation. However, the question asks for "findings most likely to be present," and sepsis is a potential complication. But reviewing the options again, none perfectly fit, but sepsis is the most plausible secondary finding.

However, the initial question lists "Glucose Insulin Insulin Receptor Responsiveness" as part of the question, which might be a hint toward diabetes-related issues. But in the options, sepsis is the one that could be associated with an infected diabetic ulcer.

But let's reconsider: the question is "which of the following findings are most likely to be present?" and the options are all acute conditions. The patient has a chronic diabetic complication, so an acute infection (sepsis) might develop.

Alternatively, option C: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is sometimes used to treat infections in diabetic foot ulcers, but an adverse effect from it (like rash or hyperkalemia) isn't directly related.

Given the context, sepsis syndrome (F) is the most likely because diabetic foot ulcers are prone to infection and can lead to sepsis.

But wait, the decreased sensation points to neuropathy, which is diabetic, and A1c 7% confirms diabetes. So the ulcer is diabetic. Sepsis is a common consequence.

Among the options, F is the best fit.

Belief: 0.85 (high confidence because diabetic foot ulcers often lead to infections and sepsis, but not absolute as other options are unrelated).
<answer>F</answer>

results.question: A 67-year-old man is evaluated in the intensive care unit. He has end-stage pancreatic cancer and was hospitalized 3 days ago for treatment of pneumonia. Respirations are 6/min. Pulse oximetry on 100% oxygen by face mask shows an oxygen saturation of 78%. Examination shows feeble respiratory efforts; he is using accessory muscles of respiration. On mental status examination, the patient is oriented to person but not to place or time. If the patient is not endotracheally intubated and mechanically ventilated, he will die within hours. His wife says the patient recently told her that he would never want mechanical ventilation, but they never completed paperwork regarding his wishes. His daughter insists that he be mechanically ventilated. Which of the following is the most appropriate action for the physician to take?
