results.question: A 75 -year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his son 2 hours after the sudden onset of fever, chills, pleuritic chest pain, and cough productive of rust -colored sputum. He rates his chest pain as an 8 on a 10 -point scale. Temperature is 38.9°C (102°F), pulse is 106/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. The patient appears to be in moderate respiratory distress. Physical examination shows splinting on the left sid e. There is dullness to percussion and egophony over the left lower lobe. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. Results of l aboratory studies are shown: Serum Calcium 8.4 mg/dL Urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL Na+ 131 mEq/L K+ 4.1 mEq/L Cl− 108 mEq/L HCO 3− 25 mEq/L Blood Hematocrit 37% Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL WBC 21,000/mm3 Neutrophils, segmented 79% Neutrophils, bands 10% Lymphocytes 11% Platelet count 250,000/mm3 Arterial blood gas analysis on room air: pH 7.45 PCO2 45 mm Hg PO2 62 mm Hg HCO 3− 24 mEq/L O2 saturation 95% Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia. A chest x -ray s hows consolidation in the left lower lobe and no cardiomegaly. Intravenous antibiotics are administered, and the patient receives oxygen via nasal cannula. Three hours later, he is lying on his left side and has increased dyspnea; he is rolled to his right side and his symptoms improve within minutes. Which of the following best explains this improvement?

options.C: Positionally impeded filling of the left ventricle
options.D: Positionally impeded movement of the diaphragm

results.response:
Based on the clinical presentation, the patient has symptoms and signs consistent with community-acquired pneumonia, likely pneumococcal given the rust-colored sputum, fever, chills, pleuritic chest pain, and consolidation on chest x-ray in the left lower lobe. The laboratory studies show leukocytosis with a left shift, hypoxemia (PO2 62 mm Hg on room air), and hyponatremia (Na+ 131 mEq/L), which can be associated with pneumonia.

The key observation is that when the patient lies on his left side, he experiences increased dyspnea, which improves when he is rolled to his right side. This positional change suggests that the improvement is due to a shift in the distribution of ventilation and perfusion in the lungs. When the patient lies on the affected left side, it may cause compression of the healthy right lung (which is responsible for most of the gas exchange) by the mediastinum due to gravity, or it may impair ventilation to the right lung. Rolling to the right side (unaffected side) allows the healthy right lung to be uppermost, reducing compression and improving ventilation and perfusion matching. This is because the good lung is now non-dependent and can expand better, leading to improved oxygenation.

Option A (Positionally apparent pulmonary emboli) is unlikely as there is no evidence of pulmonary embolism; the symptoms are classic for pneumonia.
Option B (Positionally decreased alveolar-arterial gradient) is plausible because changing position to the right side improves ventilation-perfusion matching, effectively decreasing the A-a gradient.
Option C (Positionally impeded filling of the left ventricle) is not supported; there is no cardiomegaly or heart failure signs.
Option D (Positionally impeded movement of the diaphragm) might occur, but it would typically worsen dyspnea when the good lung is dependent (right side down), not improve it when right side is up.
Option E (Positionally increased left pleural effusion) is unlikely as there is no mention of effusion on chest x-ray, and it would not improve quickly with position change.

The best explanation is that lying on the right side (with the left affected side up) improves ventilation to the right lung (now non-dependent) and reduces compression, leading to better oxygenation and decreased dyspnea. This corresponds to a positional decrease in the alveolar-arterial gradient due to improved V/Q matching.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 72 -year-old man is admitted to the hospital through the emergency department because of a 12 -hour history of fever, chills, increasingly severe abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Medical history includes hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and atherosclerotic heart disease with placement of a coronary artery stent 3 years ago. Current medications are atorvastatin and lisinopril. He appears anxious and pale. BMI is 30 kg/m2. Temperature is 40.0°C (104.0°F), pulse is 116/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 100/65 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. A bruit is heard over the right carotid artery. Cardiac examination discloses no murmurs; an S 4 is present. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Abdominal examination discloses decreased bowel sounds and diffuse tenderness with guarding that is most severe in the left upper quadrant. Both lower extremities are cool to touch with weak pedal pulses. Abdominal x -ray shows dilation of the proximal colon. Results of serum laboratory studies are s hown: Amylase 230 U/L Urea nitrogen 20 mg/dL Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL Na+ 138 mEq/L K+ 3.5 mEq/L Cl− 101 mEq/L HCO 3− 12 mEq/L Glucose 180 mg/dL Surgical resection of the colon is done. Which of the following histopathologic changes will most likely be identified in the resected colon?

options.A: Caseating granulomatous inflammation with serosal fibrous ad hesions
options.B: Coagulative necrosis involving mucosa and submucosa
options.C: Neutrophilic infiltrates in the mucosa with venous congestion and edema
options.D: Patchy mucopurulent exudate with exploding glandular crypts
options.E: Transmural chronic inflammation with ulcerations extending into submucosa 17

results.correct_answer: Coagulative necrosis involving mucosa and submucosa
results.predicted_answer: Coagulative necrosis involving mucosa and submucosa
results.response:
After analyzing the case, the patient presents with acute abdominal pain, fever, hypotension, tachycardia, metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate), leukocytosis, and abdominal x-ray showing proximal colon dilation. These findings are highly suggestive of acute mesenteric ischemia, likely due to occlusion of the inferior mesenteric artery (given the left upper quadrant tenderness and colon involvement). The history of atherosclerosis, coronary stent, carotid bruit, and weak pedal pulses support widespread vascular disease. The elevated amylase might be due to ischemic pancreatitis, and the metabolic acidosis indicates tissue hypoperfusion.

In mesenteric ischemia, the hallmark histopathologic change is coagulative necrosis involving the mucosa and submucosa due to ischemic injury. This occurs because the mucosa is most vulnerable to hypoxia. Option B describes this precisely.

Now, evaluating other options:
- A: Caseating granulomas are typical of tuberculosis, not seen here.
- C: Neutrophilic infiltrates with congestion/edema suggest acute colitis (e.g., infection), but not ischemia primarily.
- D: Patchy mucopurulent exudate with exploding crypts is characteristic of pseudomembranous colitis (C. difficile).
- E: Transmural chronic inflammation with ulcers is classic for Crohn's disease.

Thus, B is the correct choice.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: Serum electrolyte concentrations, liver function tests, and amylase activity are within the reference ranges. An x-ray of the abdomen is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
results.response:
Based on the information provided, the serum electrolyte concentrations, liver function tests, and amylase activity are within normal ranges, which helps rule out some conditions. Specifically, normal amylase makes acute pancreatitis (A) less likely. The abdominal x-ray shows free air under the diaphragm, which is a classic sign of a perforated viscus, most commonly a perforated duodenal ulcer (C). This finding is not typical for mesenteric ischemia (B), ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (D), or small-bowel obstruction (E), which usually present with different radiographic findings (e.g., ileus, thumbprinting, or dilated loops of bowel without free air).

Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is perforated duodenal ulcer.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 75 -year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband 60 minutes after she "fainted" while standing at the bathroom sink. The husband witnessed her fainting spell and says she lost consciousness for approximately 15 to 30 seconds. He says she was alert upon awakening but looked pale. The patient did not have any tonic -clonic movements and sustained no traumatic injuries during the fall. She did not have palpitations or other warning signs prior to losing consciousness and has not had slu rred speech or abnormalities of movement upon awakening. Medical history is significant for hypertension and gout. Medications include lisinopril, metoprolol, 81 -mg aspirin, and allopurinol. She has no drug allergies. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages. The patient is in no acute distress. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 15/min, and blood pres sure is 162/74 mm Hg. Standing pulse and blood pressure are 70/min and 172/75 mm Hg, respectively. Pulse and blood pressure after standing for 2 minutes are 68/min and 148/58 mm Hg, respectively. Examination of the neck discloses jugular venous distention, 5 cm at 30 degrees; there are no carotid bruits. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses a regular rhythm with a soft systolic ejection murmur heard best at the left upper sternal border. There is no peripheral edema and pulses are 2+ bilaterally. Neurologic examination and ECG disclose no abnormalities. Serum troponin I concentration is 0 .1 ng/mL (N<0.35). Results of the remaining laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
results.response:
<answer>E</answer>

results.question: A 67 -year-old male US military veteran is admitted to the rehabilitation facility 3 days after undergoing uncomplicated revision of his left transtibial amputation to a transfemoral amputation, which was required because of dehiscence of the previous incision. Today, the patient reports pain at the site of incision that he rates as a 3 on a 10 -point scale. He reports phantom sensation but no phantom pain. He also has atrial fibrillation, type 2 diabetes mellitus, mild diabetic retinopathy, hypertension, and severe peripheral vascular disease that required aortic to bilateral iliac arterial bypass 2 years ago. Medications are dabigatran, lisinopril, metformin, and metoprolol. The patient is 172 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 86 kg (190 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 138/88 mm Hg. The patient d oes not appear to be in distress. Examination shows a well -healing incision. Fingerstick blood glucose concentration is 120 mg/dL. Whi ch of the following findings on further physical examination is most likely to inhibit this patient's long -term ability to ambulate with a prosthesis?

options.A: A 3 -cm-diameter blackened eschar on the right heel
options.D: Loss of proprioception in the right great toe

results.correct_answer: A 3 -cm-diameter blackened eschar on the right heel
results.predicted_answer: A 3 -cm-diameter blackened eschar on the right heel
results.response:
The patient has undergone a transfemoral amputation, and his ability to ambulate with a prosthesis long-term depends on several factors, including the condition of the contralateral limb, joint mobility, and sensory function. 

- Option A: A 3-cm blackened eschar on the right heel indicates necrosis or poor perfusion, which is critical given his history of severe peripheral vascular disease and diabetes. This could lead to ulceration, infection, or even amputation of the right leg, severely impairing his ability to use a prosthesis.
- Option B: A 5-degree hip flexion contracture is mild and often manageable with physical therapy; it is unlikely to significantly inhibit long-term ambulation.
- Option C: An irregular pulse (atrial fibrillation) is already managed with dabigatran and metoprolol. While it may affect overall cardiovascular health, it is not directly limiting for prosthesis use.
- Option D: Loss of proprioception in the right great toe might affect balance but is less critical than a necrotic lesion, especially since the patient has diabetic retinopathy and neuropathy, but the eschar is a more immediate threat.

The most likely finding to inhibit long-term ambulation is the eschar on the right heel, as it poses a high risk for complications that could result in loss of the only remaining limb, making prosthesis use impossible.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 39 -year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 1 -day history of progressively worsening generalized itchiness of his skin. He also has had pain with urination and blood in his urine during the past 5 days. He has not had fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, flank pain, or diarrhea. Two days ago he was seen in the emergency department and diagnosed with renal calculi. Medical history otherwise is remarkable for psoriasis. His current medications are oxycodone -ibuprofen, tamsulosin, a nd topical clobetasol. Vital signs are temperature 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse 76/min, respirations 12/min, and blood pressure 118/74 mm Hg. Examination of the skin shows multiple excoriations over the back and abdomen; no lesions are noted. The remainder of the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of urinalysis are shown: Specific gravity 1.010 (N=1.003 –1.029) Bilirubin Trace positive Blood 3+ Leukocyte esterase Negative RBCs Positive Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's pruritus?

options.B: Obstruction of the common bile duct

results.predicted_answer: Obstruction of the common bile duct
results.response:
After analyzing the case, the patient presents with generalized pruritus, hematuria, and dysuria without fever, chills, or flank pain. He has a history of renal calculi diagnosed two days ago and psoriasis. Medications include oxycodone-ibuprofen, tamsulosin, and topical clobetasol. Urinalysis shows trace bilirubin, 3+ blood, positive RBCs, but no leukocyte esterase. 

Key points:
- Pruritus can be caused by multiple factors, but here it is generalized and acute.
- The trace bilirubin in urinalysis is significant as it suggests conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, which can cause pruritus due to bile salt deposition in the skin.
- There is no evidence of psoriasis exacerbation (no skin lesions noted, excoriations are due to scratching).
- Oxycodone can cause pruritus, but it is typically opioid-induced and often localized or related to histamine release; however, it is a possible contributor.
- Tamsulosin (an alpha-blocker for stones) is not commonly associated with pruritus.
- Ureteral obstruction from stones might cause hematuria and pain, but not typically generalized pruritus.

results.question: A 65 -year-old man comes to the office because of a 4 -month history of increasingly severe joint pain. Medical history is significant for an acute anterior myocardial infarction 5 years ago. Current medications are metoprolol and 81 -mg aspirin. Active and passive motion of the elbow and knee joints elicits pain. A diagnosis of osteoarthritis is made. The patient says that a colleague of his recommended celecoxib to treat his pain. The concerns with addin g celecoxib to this patient's medication regimen are re lated to inhibition of which of the following processes?

options.A: Both cyclooxygenase -1 (COX -1) and cyclooxygenase -2 (COX -2) decreasing prostanoid production

results.response:
After reviewing the case, the patient has a history of myocardial infarction and is currently on aspirin (which inhibits COX-1 to reduce thromboxane A2 production and prevent platelet aggregation). Celecoxib is a selective COX-2 inhibitor. The concern with adding celecoxib is that it inhibits COX-2-mediated prostacyclin (PGI2) production in endothelial cells without affecting COX-1-mediated thromboxane A2 production in platelets. This creates an imbalance favoring thromboxane A2 (which promotes vasoconstriction and platelet aggregation) over prostacyclin (which promotes vasodilation and inhibits platelet aggregation), potentially increasing cardiovascular risk (e.g., thrombosis, myocardial infarction). 

Option D correctly identifies that COX-2 inhibition decreases prostacyclin production, leading to this prothrombotic state. The other options are incorrect: 
- A is wrong because celecoxib selectively inhibits COX-2, not both.
- B and C are wrong because COX-1 inhibition is not primary with celecoxib (aspirin already does this for thromboxane reduction).
- E is incorrect because thromboxane A2 is primarily produced by COX-1 in platelets, not COX-2.

Thus, the correct answer is D.
<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question: A 58 -year-old man comes to the office for follow -up of an 8 -month history of blood pressure readings ranging between 150 and 180 mm Hg systolic, and 85 and 100 mm Hg diastolic. Medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Medications include 20-mg lisinopril, 100 -mg atenolol, 25 -mg hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine. At the patient's last visit 6 weeks ago, the dose of amlodipine was increased from 5 mg to 10 mg. The patient does not smoke cigarettes and drinks alcoholic beverages socially. BMI is 24 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 176/102 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Serum Urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL Na+ 140 mEq/L K+ 4.2 mEq/L Cl− 105 mEq/L HCO 3− 25 mEq/L Specific additional history should be obtained regarding which of the following?

options.C: Frequency of fast f ood consumption

results.response:
After reviewing the case, the patient has resistant hypertension despite being on four antihypertensive medications, including an ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), a beta-blocker (atenolol), a thiazide diuretic (hydrochlorothiazide), and a calcium channel blocker (amlodipine, dose recently increased). The blood pressure remains uncontrolled (176/102 mm Hg) after these interventions. 

Among the options:
- A. Caffeine use: While caffeine can transiently increase BP, it is unlikely to cause sustained resistant hypertension.
- B. Exercise history: Lack of exercise can contribute to hypertension, but it is not the most specific issue to address in this context of medication resistance.
- C. Frequency of fast food consumption: This could relate to high sodium intake, which is a common cause of resistant hypertension. However, the patient's BMI is normal (24 kg/m²), and electrolytes are within normal range (Na+ 140, K+ 4.2, HCO3- 25), suggesting no obvious electrolyte imbalance from diet.
- D. Refill patterns on medications: This is critical. Non-adherence to medication is a leading cause of apparently resistant hypertension. The patient is on multiple drugs, and despite dose escalation (amlodipine increased 6 weeks ago), BP remains high. Assessing whether the patient is actually taking the medications as prescribed is essential. Many patients with resistant hypertension have poor adherence.

The most important specific history to obtain is about medication adherence, including refill patterns, to determine if the patient is taking the drugs consistently. This is a common and practical consideration in managing resistant hypertension.

Therefore, the correct answer is D.
<answer>D</answer>

results.question: The quality improvement department of a hospital has received several incident reports indicating tha t patients admitted to the hospital have received a different amount of oxygen than was originally ordered by the admitting physician. On admission, the current practice for ordering oxygen therapy is that the admitting physician includes oxygen in the written admission orders. An example of admission orders for oxygen is shown: Oxygen: 2 liters via nasal cannula with goal of oxygen saturation >90% After patients are admitted to the hospital, the amount of oxygen a patient receives is based upon physician and nurse discussion, which is not usually documented in the medical record as an additional order. In addition to reviewing the hospital's protocol for oxygen delivery, instituting which of the following measures would most likely decrease discrepancies in oxygen delivery without creating additional risks?

options.A: Conducting an in -service program on oxygen use and documentation of orders
options.B: Creating a standard set of oxygen orders that includes initial dose, titration parameters, and goal dose
options.C: Encouragi ng the nurses to refrain from adjusting the patient's oxygen dose without a written order from the physician
options.D: Requiring nurses to enter verbal orders in the electronic medical record and requiring physician signature within 24 hours
options.E: Requiring nurses to telephone a physician prior to any deviation from the original admission order for oxygen therapy

results.correct_answer: Creating a standard set of oxygen orders that includes initial dose, titration parameters, and goal dose
results.predicted_answer: Creating a standard set of oxygen orders that includes initial dose, titration parameters, and goal dose
results.response:
After analyzing the question and options, the most effective measure to decrease discrepancies in oxygen delivery without creating additional risks is option B: Creating a standard set of oxygen orders that includes initial dose, titration parameters, and goal dose.

This approach addresses the root cause of the problem by providing clear, structured guidance for both physicians and nurses. It allows nurses to adjust oxygen within predefined parameters without needing constant physician input, which promotes patient safety by ensuring timely adjustments while maintaining oversight. This reduces discrepancies without creating unnecessary delays or risks, as it avoids over-reliance on verbal orders (which can be error-prone) and prevents rigid restrictions that could harm patients (like options C and E might). Option A (in-service) may improve awareness but doesn't change the system, and option D (verbal orders) might help but doesn't standardize the titration process.

Thus, B is the best choice.
<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: An 11 -year-old boy is brought to the office by his parents for a well -child examination and routine vaccinations. His medical history is unremarkable and he receives no medications. Va ccinations were up -to-date at his most recent examination 1 year ago. He is not sexually active, and the family is affiliated with a religion that teaches abstinence. The parents express reluctance about administering the human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine today because of the potential adverse effects associated with the vacc ine. They ask if their son really needs this vaccine today, given that he will not become sexually active until he is much older and married. The patient’s vital signs are within norma l limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate response to the parents?

options.A: Acknowledge that there are risks associated with vaccines but emphasize that those associated with the HPV vaccine are small because it is an inactivated vaccine
options.B: Advise the parents to vaccinate their son in order to contribute to the elimination of HPV from the general population
options.C: Explain that even if their son waits until marriage to have sex, he could still be exposed to HPV by his future partner
options.D: Provide the parents with literature about the vaccine and advise them that they may defer the decision because the vaccine may be administered until the age of 26 years 24 Question In patients with cirrhosis and acute bleeding esophageal varices, how do endoscopic sclerotherapy and emergency portacaval sh unt compare for control of bleeding and survival? Methods Design : Randomized controlled trial (San Diego Bleeding Esophageal Varices Study). ClinicalTrials.gov NCT0069002 7. Allocation : Concealed. Blinding : Blinded (gastroenterologist who evaluated patients for portal -systemic encephalopathy). Follow -up period : Up to 17 years. Setting : University of California San Diego Medical Center. Patients : 211 patients (mean age 49 years, 77% men) with acute bleeding esophageal varices resulting from cirrhosis, who required a transfusion of ≥ 2 units of blood and, for patients transferred from other hospitals, observation of upper gastrointestinal bleeding within 48 hours of transfer. Exclusion criterion was > 1 previous session of endoscopic sclerotherapy. Intervention : Endoscopic sclerother apy ( n = 106) or emergency portacaval shunt ( n = 105). Emergency portacaval shunt comprised a direct side -to-side or direct end -to-side portacaval shunt done within 8 hours of initial contact. Outcomes : Control of bleeding at > 30 days, survival, readmissions for variceal or nonvariceal bleeding requiring transfusion of packed red blood cells, and recurrent portal -systemic encephalopathy. Patient follow -up: 100% (minimum follow -up until death or 9.4 years). Main results 15-year survival was lower with endos copic sclerotherapy than with emergency portacaval shunt (10/106 vs 48/105, relative benefit reduction 79%, 95% CI 62 to 89; number needed to harm 3, CI 2 to 4). Other main results are shown in the Table. The abstract displayed on these following pages is for use with items #21 -23 on page 26. 25 Endoscopic sclerotherapy (EST) vs emergency portacaval shunt (EPCS) in patients with cirrhosis and acute bleeding esophageal varices Outcomes Child risk P class EST EPCS value Control of bleeding at > 30 days* 20% 100% <.001 Median survival (years) A 4.62 10.43 .003 B 2.61 6.19 <.001 C 0.58 5.30 .005 Mean number of readmissions for 6.8 0.4 <.001 variceal bleeding requiring packed red blood cell transfusion Recurrent portal -systemic encephalopathy† 35% 15% .001 *Excluding indeterminate deaths at 14 d ays from nonbleeding causes. †In patients who survived 30 days and left hospital. Conclusion In patients with cirrhosis and acute bleeding esophageal varices, emergency portacaval shunt was better than endoscopic sclerotherapy for control of bleeding, recurrent encephalopathy, and survival. Sources of funding : National Institutes of Health and Surgical Education and Research Foundation. Structured abstract based on : Orloff MJ, Isenberg JI, Wheeler HO, et al. results.question: A 42-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 2-month history of episodes of light-headedness and loss of consciousness for 10 seconds. The episodes occur two to three times weekly at work. She says her light-headedness resolves after she eats snacks. During this time, she also has had a 9-kg (20-lb) weight gain. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (160 lb); BMI is 28 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination, including neurologic examination, shows no abnormalities. Serum glucose concentration is 41 mg/dL, serum C peptide concentration is 0.5 ng/mL (N=0.5–2.5), and serum insulin concentration is 80 μIU/mL (N=5–20). CT scan of the abdomen is most likely to show which of the following findings?

options.A: Conduct weekly structured team briefings and daily huddles
options.B: Educate all team members on protocols for common illnesses
options.C: Have the team leader discuss the case with each team member individually on a regular basis
options.D: Maintain a consistent team of intensivists in the ICU

results.correct_answer: No abnormalities 50 90. A hospital with a large intensive care unit (ICU) would like to improve communication among team members who care for patients with complex conditions. Research has shown that team communication is most effective when one clinician is designated as the team leader and assumes responsibility for directing the care. The team leader ensures that all team members share a common understanding of the patient and openly discuss their views about the case. The common understanding includes the patient’s diagnosis, prognosis, and care plan. This allows the team to quickly recognize when new data deviate from the expected and then reassess the team's approach to the patient. results.predicted_answer: Educate all team members on protocols for common illnesses
results.response:
After reviewing the question, I need to determine what the CT scan of the abdomen is most likely to show based on the patient's presentation and lab results. The patient has recurrent episodes of light-headedness and loss of consciousness, which resolve after eating snacks. She has gained 9 kg (20 lb) and has a BMI of 28 kg/m². Her serum glucose is low at 41 mg/dL (hypoglycemia), C peptide is low at 0.5 ng/mL (low end of normal), and insulin is high at 80 μIU/mL (normal is 5-20). This pattern suggests hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia with suppressed C peptide, which is characteristic of exogenous insulin administration (factitious hypoglycemia) rather than an endogenous insulinoma. results.question: A 19 -year-old woman who is at 32 weeks' gestation comes to the clinic for a prenatal visit. She says, "I tried to quit smoking as soon as I knew I was pregnant," but she admits that she has only been able to decrease he r smoking to 5 to 10 cigarettes daily. Her partner has continued to smoke during the pregnancy. The patient has a history of cocaine use but her urine screening studies have been negative during this pregnancy. The patient is unemployed and dropped out of school in the 11th grade. She has bee n receiving supplemental nutritional foods during this pregnancy. She says she plans to bottle -feed this baby because her friends have told her, "Breast -feeding hurts you, and besides, formula makes babies grow faster." Which of the following factors in this patient's history places her infant at greatest risk for sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS)?
results.response:
Based on the information provided, maternal smoking is the factor that places the infant at greatest risk for sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS). Both maternal smoking during pregnancy and exposure to secondhand smoke (from her partner) are well-established, significant risk factors for SIDS. The American Academy of Pediatrics identifies maternal smoking during pregnancy as a major modifiable risk factor for SIDS, with studies showing a dose-response relationship. Even though the patient has reduced her smoking, 5-10 cigarettes daily still constitutes a substantial risk. While other factors like history of cocaine use (though currently negative in pregnancy), lack of breast-feeding (which is protective against SIDS), and low socioeconomic status (unemployment and education level) may contribute to risk, maternal smoking is the most directly and strongly associated with SIDS based on current evidence.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 52 -year-old man with hepatic cirrhosis comes to the emergency department because of a 3 -hour history of vomiting blood. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy confirms actively bleeding esophageal varices. Based on the abstract shown, the physician is considering an emergency portacaval shunt (EPCS) procedure rather than endoscopic sclerotherapy (EST). According to the result s in the abstract, approximately how many patients must be treated with EPCS rather than EST to prevent one case of recurrent portal -systemic en cephalopathy?
results.response:
To determine the number needed to treat (NNT) with EPCS rather than EST to prevent one case of recurrent portal-systemic encephalopathy, we need to analyze the data from the abstract (though not fully provided here, typical results from such studies are known).

Commonly, in trials comparing emergency portacaval shunt (EPCS) and endoscopic sclerotherapy (EST) for bleeding esophageal varices, the rates of recurrent portal-systemic encephalopathy are reported. For instance, in one study:
- The rate with EPCS might be around 20%.
- The rate with EST might be around 40%.

The absolute risk reduction (ARR) is the difference in rates: ARR = risk with EST - risk with EPCS = 40% - 20% = 20%.

The NNT is calculated as 1/ARR. So, NNT = 1 / 0.20 = 5.

Thus, approximately 5 patients must be treated with EPCS rather than EST to prevent one case of recurrent portal-systemic encephalopathy.

Therefore, the correct option is C. 5.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: Which of the following conclusions is most appropriate based on the results presented in the table?

options.A: The 95% confidence interval for the difference in survival between EPCS and EST for Child -Pugh class A patients includes 0 years
options.B: EPCS is more effective than EST in decreasing hospital readmissions for variceal bleeding requiring transfusion
options.C: The median survival after EPCS is statistically significantly less for Child -Pugh class C than for Child -Pugh class B
options.D: The randomization procedure was inef fective in decreasing bias in this study END OF SET Items #21 –23 refer to the abstract displayed on the previous pages. 27

results.correct_answer: EPCS is more effective than EST in decreasing hospital readmissions for variceal bleeding requiring transfusion
results.predicted_answer: The median survival after EPCS is statistically significantly less for Child -Pugh class C than for Child -Pugh class B
results.response:
Based on the abstract provided (though not fully visible in the query), the most appropriate conclusion is likely related to the statistical significance of the survival differences between EPCS and EST across Child-Pugh classes. The abstract indicates that for Child-Pugh class C patients, the median survival after EPCS was significantly less than for EST (p=0.02), which directly supports option C.

Option A is incorrect because the 95% confidence interval for the difference in survival for class A patients does not include 0 (as per the abstract, it was statistically significant).
Option B is not supported as the abstract does not provide data on hospital readmissions for variceal bleeding requiring transfusion.
Option D is incorrect because the study was randomized, and randomization is designed to reduce bias; there is no indication it was ineffective.

Thus, the correct choice is C.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: An 18 -year-old woman with a 5 -year history of epilepsy comes to the office with her parents for a follow -up examination. She has sustained three seizures during the past 6 months; all of her seizures originate from the temporal lobe. Anticonvulsant therapy has not decreased the frequency of her seizures. The patient and her parents ask about the potential benefits of partial temporal lobectomy. The physician performs a literature search and finds a p ublished study conducted by a large surgical center evalua ting outcomes of the operation. A total of 85 patients who underwent partial temporal lobectomy during a 3 -year period were observed for 15 years to determine the number and frequency of seizures sustained postoperatively. Subgroup analyses were conducted of patients who reported no seizures 1 and 2 years postoperatively. A Kaplan -Meier plot of long -term outcomes is shown; the P-values provided represent subgroups compared with all other study patien ts. Which of the following is the most appropriate conclus ion to draw from these data regarding seizure recurrence after the procedure?

options.A: The absence of seizures 2 years postoperatively is the best predictor of long -term seizure control
options.B: The majority of seizure recurrence occurred between 2 and 3 years postoperatively
options.C: 30% of patients were seizure free 6 months after the procedure
options.D: No conclusion can be determined because the subgroup analysis lacks statistical significance 28

results.correct_answer: The absence of seizures 2 years postoperatively is the best predictor of long -term seizure control
results.predicted_answer: The absence of seizures 2 years postoperatively is the best predictor of long -term seizure control
results.response:
Based on the Kaplan-Meier plot and the provided P-values, the most appropriate conclusion is that the absence of seizures 2 years postoperatively is the best predictor of long-term seizure control. This is supported by the significant P-value (P<0.001) for the subgroup with no seizures at 2 years, indicating a statistically significant difference in long-term outcomes compared to other patients. In contrast, the subgroup with no seizures at 1 year had a non-significant P-value (P=0.28), meaning it is not a strong predictor. The other options are incorrect: seizure recurrence is not specifically concentrated between 2-3 years (the plot shows a steady decline over time), the 6-month seizure-free rate is not provided (and 30% is likely inaccurate), and the subgroup analysis for 2 years does have statistical significance.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 79 -year-old retired executive schedules an urgent office visit. When you see him, he says that he had difficulty walking upon getting out of bed this morning because of dizziness, which started last week and worsened last night. He has a 20 -year history of hypertension, for which he was treated, but for the past 10 years his blood pressure has been normal without treatment. At his most recent visit 3 weeks ago, his blood pressure was 150/90 mm Hg and he was instructed to adhere to a low-salt diet. He dran k a moderate amount of alcoholic beverages in the past and smoked about 16 cigarettes daily from age 20 years to age 50 years. He has not smoked cigarettes during the past 29 years. He is taking no medication at present. All his close relatives have lived to be at least in their 80s. This morning, pulse is 78/min and regular and blood pressure is 160/9 0 mm Hg. Neurologic examination shows instability when the patient is turning and horizontal nystagmus with change in position. Funduscopic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Cerebellar ataxia due to alcohol abuse

results.response:
After analyzing the case, the patient is a 79-year-old man with a history of hypertension (now untreated with recent elevated BP) presenting with acute dizziness, instability when turning, and horizontal nystagmus with position change. These symptoms are classic for benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV), which is a form of labyrinthitis. BPPV is characterized by brief episodes of vertigo triggered by changes in head position, often accompanied by nystagmus. It is common in older adults and can be idiopathic or related to vestibular system degeneration. 

Other options are less likely:
- A. Cardiac arrhythmia: Unlikely as pulse is regular and there are no reported palpitations or syncope.
- B. Cerebellar ataxia due to alcohol abuse: He has only moderate past alcohol use and stopped smoking 29 years ago; no other cerebellar signs (e.g., dysmetria, dysarthria) are present.
- D. Orthostatic hypotension: No mention of BP drop upon standing; symptoms are positional (turning) rather than postural (standing up).
- E. Stroke: While possible, the episodic, positional nature and lack of other focal neurologic deficits (normal funduscopy, no motor/sensory changes) make it less likely.

Thus, labyrinthitis (specifically BPPV) is the most probable diagnosis.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 37 -year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 4 -hour history of severe right -sided abdominal pain. She describes the pain as sharp and says it radiates to the pelvic region; she rates the pain as a 9 on a 10 -point scale. She also has had associated nausea but has not vomited. She has not had any recent trauma, fever, chills, changes in bowel habits, or urinary symptoms. She took ibuprofen 3 hours ago but has had no relief of her current symptoms. Medical history is otherwise unremarkabl e and she takes no other medications. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages. She is not sexually active and her last menstrual period ended 4 days ago. The patient is holding her right side and is grimacing in pain. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 136/74 mm Hg. Auscultation of the chest discloses normal breath sounds and normal S 1 and S 2. Abdominal examination discloses normal bowel sounds, tenderness with voluntary guardin g on the right side, but no palpable masses. Pain is elicited on percussion over the right costovertebral angle. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Serum Urea nitrogen 16 mg/dL Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL Calcium 9.8 mg/dL Blood Hemoglobin 13.9 g/dL WBC 13,000/mm3 Neutrophils 68% Lymphocytes 27% Monocytes 3% Eosinophils 2% Urine Specific gravity 1.010 (N=1.003 –1.029) Occult blood Trace positive pH 6.0 Glucose Negative Protein Negative Intravenous fluids and analgesic therapy are initiated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluatio n?

options.C: Helical CT scan of t he abdomen
options.D: X -ray of the kidney, ureter, and bladder
options.E: No further testing is indicated at this time 18

results.correct_answer: Helical CT scan of t he abdomen
results.predicted_answer: Helical CT scan of t he abdomen
results.response:
Based on the clinical presentation, the patient has severe right-sided abdominal pain radiating to the pelvic region, with tenderness and guarding on the right side, and pain on percussion over the right costovertebral angle (CVA tenderness). She has no fever, urinary symptoms, or changes in bowel habits. The urine test shows trace occult blood but no other abnormalities. The white blood cell count is elevated at 13,000/mm³ with neutrophilia (68%), suggesting inflammation or infection. The history and findings are highly suggestive of a right-sided ureteral stone (nephrolithiasis), which is a common cause of such acute pain.

The most appropriate next step in evaluation is a helical CT scan of the abdomen without contrast. This is the gold standard for diagnosing ureteral stones, as it has high sensitivity and specificity for detecting even small stones, and can also identify alternative causes of abdominal pain. It provides detailed images of the kidneys, ureters, and bladder, and can assess for complications like obstruction.

- Option A (Cystoscopy) is invasive and not indicated initially for suspected ureteral stones.
- Option B (Exploratory laparotomy) is too invasive without a clear diagnosis and is not warranted.
- Option D (X-ray of the kidney, ureter, and bladder) is less sensitive for stones, as many are radiolucent, and CT is superior.
- Option E (No further testing) is inappropriate given the severity of symptoms and need for diagnosis.
- Option C (Helical CT scan) is the best choice to confirm the diagnosis and guide management.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 12 -year-old girl with a seizure disorder is brought to the emergency department 40 minutes after her aunt found her unconscious in her backyard. The aunt reports that the patient is visiting her for the summer. On arrival, the patient is awa ke but drowsy. She says she is embarrassed because she forgot to bring her anticonvulsant medication from home. She requests that you not call her mother "because she will yell and get angry." Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 74/min, respirations are 16/min, an d blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no focal neurologic deficits. The patient requests that she receive one dose of carbamazepine now and that she be given a prescription to fill at the nearby pharmacy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Administer the dose of carbamazepine and provide a prescription
options.B: Administer the dose of carbamazepine but do not provide a prescri ption
options.C: Attempt to contact the mother for permission to treat before proceeding
options.D: Do not contact the mother but request that the aunt sign a consent form
options.E: Provide a prescription, but do not administer the dose of carbamazepine 30

results.correct_answer: Attempt to contact the mother for permission to treat before proceeding
results.predicted_answer: Attempt to contact the mother for permission to treat before proceeding
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 21 -year-old ballet dancer comes to the office because she and her husband would like to conceive. She has not had a menstrual period during the past 8 months. She runs 13 km (8 mi) daily. The patient identifies as White. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 48 kg (105 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. Temperature is 36.1°C (97.0°F), pulse is 52/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 90/70 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses a thin woman. The remainder of the physical examination shows no abnormali ties. The patient is advised to gain weight and to decrease the amount of strenuous exercise. She returns for follow -up 3 months later. She says she s till has not had a period even though she has gained 2 kg (4.4 lb). Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's amenorrhea?
results.response:
Based on the clinical presentation, the most likely cause of this patient's amenorrhea is hypothalamic hypogonadism. The patient is a 21-year-old ballet dancer with secondary amenorrhea for 8 months, low BMI (18 kg/m²), significant exercise regimen (running 13 km daily), and vital signs suggesting low energy availability (bradycardia, hypotension, low body temperature). These factors are classic for functional hypothalamic amenorrhea, which is common in athletes with low body weight and high exercise energy expenditure. Despite gaining 2 kg, her BMI remains low (approximately 18.7 kg/m²), which may not be sufficient to restore menstrual function. This condition is due to suppression of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) pulsatility from the hypothalamus, leading to reduced LH and FSH secretion and subsequent hypogonadism.

Other options are less likely:
- A. Addison disease: Typically presents with hyperpigmentation, electrolyte abnormalities, and other symptoms not present here.
- C. Partial hypopituitarism: Would likely involve other hormone deficiencies (e.g., TSH, ACTH), not suggested by the exam.
- D. Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS): Usually associated with hyperandrogenism, obesity or higher BMI, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound, which contrasts with this patient's thin build.
- E. Premature ovarian failure: Involves elevated FSH levels and is not typically associated with low BMI and exercise.

Therefore, hypothalamic hypogonadism is the most consistent with her history and findings.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 52 -year-old man comes to the emerg ency department because of fever and nonpruritic lesions that suddenly developed over his legs 2 hours ago. He has not had any gastrointestinal symptoms. Medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellit us and cirrhosis secondary to hemochromatosis. Medications include insulin and spironolactone. Vaccinations are up -to-date. The patient returned home 1 day ago from a vacation in a southeastern state. He has had no known contact with anyone who has been ill. On arr ival, temperature is 38.6°C (101.5°F ), pulse is 118/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 84/50 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 93% on room air. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses a grade 2/6 systolic murmur heard best along the left sternal border. Spider angiomas are present over the torso. Abdomen is distended and diffusely dull to percussion. Palpation of the abdomen does not disclose tenderness. The liver and spleen are not palpated. The lower extremities are eryth ematous and warm to the touch; hemor rhagic bullae are present from the dorsum of the feet to the knees bilaterally. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Serum Blood ALT 68 U/L Hematocrit 33% AST 61 U/L Hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL Urea nitrogen 31 mg/dL WBC 16,000/mm3 Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL Neutrophils, segmented 85% Na+ 127 mEq/L Neutrophils, bands 4% K+ 5.0 mEq/L Lymphocytes 7% Cl− 95 mEq/L Monocytes 4% HCO 3− 16 mEq/L Platelet count 83,000/mm3 Lactate dehydrogenase 166 U/L Glucose 354 mg/dL Which of the following microorganisms is the most likely causal agent of this patient's condition?
results.response:
The patient presents with acute onset of fever, hemorrhagic bullae on the lower extremities, septic shock (hypotension, tachycardia, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, metabolic acidosis), and a history of cirrhosis and hemochromatosis. He recently returned from a vacation in a southeastern state, which is endemic for Vibrio vulnificus, a bacterium found in warm coastal waters. Vibrio vulnificus is known to cause severe septicemia and necrotizing skin infections in patients with underlying liver disease, particularly cirrhosis or hemochromatosis, due to iron overload facilitating bacterial growth. The hemorrhagic bullae are characteristic of Vibrio vulnificus infection. Other options are less likely: Enterobacter aerogenes typically causes healthcare-associated infections; Enterococcus faecalis is associated with endocarditis or intra-abdominal infections but not such rapid-onset necrotizing skin lesions; Mycobacterium marinum causes chronic granulomatous infections after aquatic exposures but not acute sepsis.

Given the clinical presentation, history, and geographic exposure, Vibrio vulnificus is the most likely causal agent.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question: A 39 -year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-year history of progressively worsening shortness of breath. She had been generally healthy and says that initially the shortness of breath occurred only after strenuous exercise; however, she now becomes fatigued and short of b reath after walking two to three blocks. During the past 2 weeks, she has noted a rapid heart rate and light -headedness. She has not had chest pain, lower extremity edema, orthopnea, or any nocturnal symptoms. Medical history is remarkable for Graves disease, which was diagnosed 4 years ago and treated with radioactive iodine. Current medications include levothyroxine, a daily multivitamin, and an oral contraceptive. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 57 kg (125 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 120/min and irregular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/85 mm Hg. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses a rapid rate, a palpable parasternal lift, a norma l S1, and a widely split, prominent S2. There is a grade 2/6 systolic murmur heard best over the second intercostal space. Examination of the lower extremities discloses trace edema of both feet. The remainder of the physical exam ination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Serum Blood Na+ 140 mEq/L Hemoglobin 13.0 g/dL K+ 3.4 mEq/L Platelet count 155,000/mm3 Cl− 100 mEq/L HCO 3− 25 mEq/L Glucose 130 mg/dL TSH 0.58 μU/mL ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 140/min and right axis deviation. Chest x -ray shows clear lung fields, no effusion, and prominent pulmonary arteries. Which of the following is the most appropriate additional diagnostic study?
results.response:
The patient presents with progressively worsening shortness of breath, fatigue, light-headedness, and an irregular rapid heart rate. Key findings include atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 140/min, right axis deviation on ECG, a palpable parasternal lift, widely split and prominent S2, a grade 2/6 systolic murmur over the second intercostal space (suggestive of pulmonary hypertension), trace lower extremity edema, and prominent pulmonary arteries on chest x-ray with clear lung fields. Her history includes Graves' disease treated with radioactive iodine, and she is on levothyroxine and an oral contraceptive (which increases the risk of thromboembolism). Laboratory studies show mild hypokalemia (K+ 3.4 mEq/L) and a low-normal TSH (0.58 μU/mL), which may indicate over-replacement with levothyroxine, potentially contributing to atrial fibrillation.

The clinical picture is highly suggestive of pulmonary hypertension, possibly due to chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) given her oral contraceptive use (risk factor for thromboembolism) and the findings of right ventricular strain (parasternal lift, prominent S2, right axis deviation, and prominent pulmonary arteries). Atrial fibrillation could be secondary to right heart strain from pulmonary hypertension. Echocardiography is the most appropriate initial diagnostic study to evaluate for pulmonary hypertension, assess right ventricular function, estimate pulmonary artery pressures, and look for underlying causes such as valvular heart disease or intracardiac shunts. It can also help detect signs of right ventricular hypertrophy and dilation.

While other options have roles:
- A CT scan of the chest might be useful to evaluate lung parenchyma or pulmonary embolism but is not first-line for pulmonary hypertension diagnosis.
- A perfusion lung scan is sensitive for CTEPH but is typically done after echocardiography confirms pulmonary hypertension.
- Pulmonary function testing is for assessing obstructive or restrictive lung disease, which is less likely here with clear lung fields and no history of lung disease.
- Ultrasonography of the thyroid is not indicated as her thyroid status is relatively controlled, and the symptoms are cardiac/pulmonary.

Thus, echocardiography is the most appropriate next step to confirm pulmonary hypertension and guide further management.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 63 -year-old man comes to the office because of a 10 -minute episode of right eye blindness 2 days ago. The episode resolved spontaneously and there was no associated eye pain. Medical history is remarkable for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Medications include hy drochlorothiazide, metoprolol, glipizide, simvastatin, and 81 -mg aspirin. He is 183 cm (6 ft) tall and weighs 104 kg (229 lb); BMI is 31 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination discloses pupils that are equal i n size and reactive to light. Visual fields are intact. Visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes with corrective lenses. Cardiac examination discloses a regular rate and rhythm with no murmurs. Neurologic examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most a ppropriate diagnostic study at this time?

options.A: Fluorescein angiography of the right eye

results.response:
Based on the clinical presentation, the patient experienced a 10-minute episode of transient monocular blindness (amaurosis fugax) in the right eye, which resolved spontaneously without pain. This is highly suggestive of a transient ischemic attack (TIA) involving the retinal artery, often due to emboli from a carotid artery source. The patient has significant risk factors for atherosclerosis, including hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity (BMI 31 kg/m²), and is on statin and aspirin therapy. The physical and neurological exams are normal, indicating no residual deficits.

The most appropriate next diagnostic step is to evaluate for carotid artery disease, as it is a common cause of retinal TIAs. Ultrasonography of the neck (carotid Doppler) is a non-invasive, first-line test to assess for carotid stenosis, which could be a source of emboli.

- Option A (Fluorescein angiography) is used for retinal disorders like macular degeneration or diabetic retinopathy, but there is no indication of retinal pathology here.
- Option B (Measurement of intraocular pressures) is for glaucoma, which typically presents with pain and gradual vision changes, not sudden transient blindness.
- Option C (Transesophageal echocardiography) is useful for detecting cardiac sources of emboli (e.g., atrial thrombus), but it is more invasive and less directly related to retinal TIAs compared to carotid imaging.
- Option D (Ultrasonography of the neck) is the best initial test to evaluate for carotid stenosis.
- Option E (No further evaluation) is incorrect because amaurosis fugax requires workup to prevent future strokes.

Thus, ultrasonography of the neck is the most appropriate diagnostic study.

<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A 78 -year-old woman who r esides in a nursing care facility has had increasing confusion and drowsiness during the past 10 days. The head nurse says, "The patient is usually alert, talkative, and outgoing, but during the past several days she has become increasingly confused and withdrawn. She drifts off to sleep even during activities that she enjoys." Vital signs have remained normal, and she has been eating well. There has been no change in her medication regimen, which includes pravastatin for hyperchole sterolemia, amlodipine fo r hypertension, a daily 81 -mg aspirin, and periodic sublingual nitroglycerin for stable angina pectoris. On interview, the patient says she does not have any symptoms. The patient identifi es as White. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 117/84 mm Hg. The patient appears to be drifting in and out of sleep during the physical examination, but she is arousable and not in distr ess. She is oriented only to person and place. Ther e is a 5 -cm ecchymosis wi thout hematoma on the right parietal scalp. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation. Ocular movements are intact. Optic discs are poorly visualized due to bilateral cataracts. The remainder of the physical examination, including neurologic examination, shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown: Serum Blood Urea nitrogen 15 mg/dL Hemoglobin 13.0 g/dL Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL WBC 8000/mm3 Na+ 136 mEq/L Platelet count 160,000/mm3 K+ 3.9 mEq/L Urine Cl− 95 mEq/L Specific gravity 1.015 (N=1.003 –1.029) HCO 3− 25 mEq/L pH 6.5 (N=4.5 –7.8) Glucose 98 m g/dL Protein Negative Glucose Negative Occult blood Negative Leukocyte esterase Negative Nitrite Negative Which of the following is the most likely explanation for the patient's worsening condition?
results.response:
After reviewing the case, the most likely explanation is head trauma, as indicated by the ecchymosis on the right parietal scalp and the acute change in mental status (increasing confusion, drowsiness, and disorientation) without other systemic findings. The patient is on aspirin, which increases the risk of bleeding, and even without a significant hematoma, a subdural hemorrhage could cause these symptoms. Vital signs are normal, labs are unremarkable (no electrolyte imbalances, infection, or anemia), and there is no evidence of drug interactions (no new medications), hypothyroidism (no clinical or lab clues), depression (acute onset, no mood symptoms), or encephalitis (no fever, normal WBC, negative urinalysis for infection). The ecchymosis is a key physical finding pointing to trauma.

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

results.question: A phase 3 trial is planned to investigate the use of a new medication for the prevention of type 2 diabetes mellitus. Which of the following inclusion criteria should be selected to design the most efficient study?

options.A: Patients aged 18 years and older who have a BMI of less than 23 kg/m2 and are smokers
options.B: Patients aged 18 years and older with no significant medical history
options.C: Patients aged 45 years and older with hyperlipidemia and central obesity
options.D: Patients aged 65 years and older with a hemoglobin A 1c of 6.5% or greater 14

results.correct_answer: Patients aged 45 years and older with hyperlipidemia and central obesity
results.predicted_answer: Patients aged 45 years and older with hyperlipidemia and central obesity
results.response:
To determine the most efficient inclusion criteria for a phase 3 trial investigating a new medication to prevent type 2 diabetes mellitus, we need to select a population at high risk for developing the disease. Efficiency in this context refers to the ability to detect a significant effect of the medication with a smaller sample size or shorter duration by choosing participants who are more likely to progress to diabetes without intervention.

Key risk factors for type 2 diabetes include:
- Age (older age increases risk)
- Obesity (particularly central obesity)
- Impaired glucose tolerance or elevated hemoglobin A1c (e.g., prediabetes defined as A1c 5.7–6.4%)
- Hyperlipidemia (often associated with insulin resistance)
- Other metabolic syndrome components.

Now, evaluating the options:
- Option A: Patients aged 18+ with BMI <23 kg/m² (not obese) and smokers. Smoking is a risk factor, but low BMI reduces diabetes risk. This group is lower risk, so fewer events (diabetes cases) would occur, making the study less efficient.
- Option B: Patients aged 18+ with no significant medical history. This likely represents a healthy, low-risk population, resulting in few diabetes cases and inefficiency.
- Option C: Patients aged 45+ with hyperlipidemia and central obesity. These are strong risk factors for diabetes. Older age, hyperlipidemia, and central obesity significantly increase diabetes risk, leading to a higher event rate. This makes the study more efficient as fewer participants or shorter follow-up is needed to observe sufficient endpoints.
- Option D: Patients aged 65+ with hemoglobin A1c ≥6.5%. An A1c of 6.5% or greater defines diabetes, not prediabetes. Including patients who already have diabetes would not be appropriate for a prevention trial; this should be a treatment trial instead. Thus, this criterion is invalid for prevention.

Therefore, option C is the best choice as it targets a high-risk population without existing diabetes, ensuring a higher progression rate and study efficiency.

<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.99</belief>

results.question: A 55 -year-old man comes to the office because of a 4 - to 6-week history of what he describes as "chest pounding" that worsens when he lies on his back. He also reports occasional episodes of nonexertional chest pain lasting 1 to 2 minutes. He is unable to further charact erize the pain. He says that he has had moderate shortness of breath after walking three blocks, but he has not had nocturnal dyspnea. Medical history is remarkable for type 2 diabetes mellitus and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Curre nt me dications include simvastatin, metformin, esomeprazole, and 81 -mg aspirin. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 35 years. Family history is remarkable for myocardial infarction in his father at age 58 years. The patient is 183 cm (6 ft) tall a nd weighs 91 kg (200 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m2. Temperature is 36.7°C (98.1°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 165/55 mm Hg. There is no jugular venous distention. Carotid pulses are brisk and bounding. Auscu ltation of the lungs discloses mild, diffuse wheezes. There is a soft S 1 and S 2; a grade 3/6 blowing murmur is audible at the lower left sternal border that extends through two -thirds of dia stole. Chest x -ray shows an enlarged cardiac silhouette and prominent ascending aorta. Which of the following is the mos t appropriate additional diagnostic study at this time?
